centuries.label: Pre-CE Middle East (3000 BCE – 1 BCE)
centuries.summary: The ancient Middle East is the cradle of civilization, writing, law, and monotheism. The region saw the rise of Sumer, Babylon, Egypt, Assyria, and Persia — and gave birth to the Hebrew scriptures that would shape Judaism, Christianity, and Islam.

facts.fact: Around 3100 BCE, the Sumerians of Mesopotamia invented the earliest known writing system — cuneiform — pressing wedge-shaped marks into clay tablets to track grain and livestock.

The Epic of Gilgamesh, written around 2100 BCE, is the world's oldest surviving literary work. It includes a flood narrative strikingly similar to the later story of Noah — predating Genesis by over a thousand years.

Hammurabi, king of Babylon (~1792–1750 BCE), produced one of history's first written legal codes. Its 282 laws introduced the principle of proportional justice: 'an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth.'

The Egyptians built the Great Pyramid of Giza around 2560 BCE — the tallest man-made structure in the world for nearly 4,000 years. Contrary to popular myth, it was built by paid laborers, not slaves.
The Hittites of Anatolia (~1600–1180 BCE) were among the first peoples to master iron smelting, giving them a military advantage that reshaped the ancient Near East.
The Hebrew Exodus story, if historical, is generally dated to around 1250 BCE under Pharaoh Ramesses II — though no Egyptian records confirm a mass exodus of Israelites.

The Assyrian Empire (~900–612 BCE) was the ancient world's first true military superpower, using iron weapons, siege engines, and systematic deportation of conquered peoples to control a vast territory from Egypt to Iran.
In 586 BCE, the Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar destroyed Solomon's Temple in Jerusalem and deported the Jewish elite to Babylon — a traumatic event that transformed Israelite religion into Judaism.

Cyrus the Great of Persia conquered Babylon in 539 BCE and famously freed the Jewish exiles, allowing them to return to Judea. He is the only non-Israelite called 'messiah' (anointed one) in the Hebrew Bible (Isaiah 45:1).

The Persian Achaemenid Empire (~550–330 BCE), at its height under Darius I, stretched from Greece to India — the largest empire the world had yet seen, administered through a remarkably tolerant policy of local autonomy.

Alexander the Great conquered the Persian Empire between 334–323 BCE, spreading Greek language and culture (Hellenism) across the Middle East — a cultural shift that would directly shape the world in which the New Testament was written.

After Alexander's death, his empire split among his generals. Judea fell under the Seleucid dynasty, whose ruler Antiochus IV Epiphanes desecrated the Jerusalem Temple in 167 BCE, sparking the Maccabean Revolt.
The Maccabean Revolt (167–160 BCE) led to a brief period of Jewish independence under the Hasmonean dynasty. The victory is celebrated in the Jewish festival of Hanukkah.
By 63 BCE, the Roman general Pompey captured Jerusalem, ending Jewish independence. Judea became a client state of Rome — the political backdrop for the entire New Testament.

The Dead Sea Scrolls, discovered in 1947, contain Hebrew manuscripts from roughly 250 BCE–70 CE — the oldest known copies of the Hebrew Bible and evidence of a diverse, sectarian Judaism that is often flattened in later tradition.

Bart Ehrman emphasizes that the Hebrew Bible was not written as a unified document — it was composed by many authors over nearly a thousand years, edited and re-edited, with contradictions that reflect evolving theological ideas rather than a single consistent theology.

The concept of a suffering, dying-and-rising god appears across ancient Middle Eastern religions — in the myths of Osiris (Egypt), Tammuz (Babylon), and later Baal (Canaan) — providing a broader religious context for understanding early Christian claims about Jesus.

Zoroastrianism, the religion of Persia, introduced concepts of cosmic dualism (good vs. evil), a final judgment, heaven and hell, and a future savior figure (Saoshyant) — ideas that heavily influenced late Jewish apocalypticism and, through it, early Christianity.

The Septuagint — a Greek translation of the Hebrew scriptures — was produced in Alexandria, Egypt around 250 BCE. It became the Bible of the early church, and many New Testament authors quote it rather than the original Hebrew.

By the 1st century BCE, Judaism was not a monolithic religion. Groups included Pharisees (focused on oral law), Sadducees (priestly aristocracy), Essenes (apocalyptic separatists), and Zealots (revolutionary nationalists) — a diversity Ehrman stresses as essential context for understanding Jesus.

centuries.label: 1st Century CE (1–99 CE)
centuries.summary: The first century CE is dominated by the Roman Empire at its height, the life of Jesus, the birth of Christianity, and the catastrophic Jewish-Roman War ending in the destruction of the Temple. Bart Ehrman's work is especially rich for this period.

facts.fact: Jesus of Nazareth was most likely born between 6–4 BCE (before Herod the Great's death in 4 BCE) — meaning the common era calendar, established centuries later, is off by several years.

Bart Ehrman argues that Jesus was an apocalyptic Jewish preacher who believed God was about to intervene in history and establish a kingdom on earth — not the founder of a new religion, but a reformer within Judaism.

The four Gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke, John) were not written by eyewitnesses. Ehrman dates them: Mark (~65–70 CE), Matthew and Luke (~80–85 CE), and John (~90–95 CE) — all decades after Jesus's death.
Mark is the earliest Gospel and the source used by Matthew and Luke — a scholarly consensus called the 'Two-Source Hypothesis.' John is independent and theologically the most developed.
Jesus was crucified under the Roman prefect Pontius Pilate around 30–33 CE. Crucifixion was a Roman punishment specifically for political troublemakers and slaves — not a Jewish execution method.

Paul of Tarsus wrote his letters in the 50s CE — before any of the Gospels were written. His letters are the earliest Christian documents we have, and his theology (salvation through faith, not works of the law) shaped Christianity more than any other single author.

Ehrman points out that Paul never met the living Jesus and rarely quotes his teachings. Paul's Christ is a cosmic figure — pre-existent, crucified, resurrected — which differs in emphasis from the earthly Jesus of the Synoptic Gospels.
In 66 CE, Jewish revolutionaries launched a revolt against Rome. The Romans responded with overwhelming force under General Vespasian and his son Titus.

In 70 CE, Roman forces under Titus destroyed the Second Temple in Jerusalem — an event so catastrophic it reshaped both Judaism and Christianity forever. Judaism became a synagogue-based, text-centered religion; Christianity increasingly separated from its Jewish roots.
The Arch of Titus in Rome, still standing today, depicts Roman soldiers carrying the Temple menorah as war spoils — a vivid monument to the 70 CE destruction.

The Roman historian Tacitus (writing ~116 CE) confirms that a man called Christus was executed by Pontius Pilate during the reign of Tiberius — one of only two non-Christian Roman references to Jesus.

Josephus, a Jewish historian writing in the 90s CE, mentions Jesus twice. One passage (the 'Testimonium Flavianum') was almost certainly edited by later Christian scribes — Ehrman and most scholars consider the current version partially interpolated.

The book of Revelation was written around 95 CE during the reign of Emperor Domitian. Tom Holland (The Rest is History) emphasizes it as a coded political document aimed at Rome, not a literal prediction of the modern future.
Emperor Augustus (ruled 27 BCE–14 CE) transformed Rome from a republic into an empire. His reign established the 'Pax Romana' — two centuries of relative peace across the Mediterranean world.
Augustus used religion as statecraft: he was declared 'Son of God' (divi filius) after Julius Caesar's deification — a title that early Christians deliberately echoed in calling Jesus 'Son of God.'
Nero (ruled 54–68 CE) became the first emperor to officially persecute Christians, blaming them for the Great Fire of Rome in 64 CE. This persecution likely claimed the lives of both Peter and Paul.

The year 69 CE is called the 'Year of the Four Emperors' — after Nero's suicide, Rome descended into civil war with four men claiming the throne in a single year, before Vespasian stabilized the empire.
Pompeii and Herculaneum were destroyed by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius on August 24, 79 CE — an event described in vivid detail by Pliny the Younger in letters that survive today.
The Colosseum (Flavian Amphitheatre) was completed in 80 CE under Emperor Titus. It could hold 50,000–80,000 spectators and hosted gladiatorial games, animal hunts, and public executions.
By the end of the 1st century, Christianity had spread across the Roman Empire — from Jerusalem to Antioch, Alexandria, Corinth, and Rome — but remained a tiny, often persecuted minority sect.

centuries.label: 2nd Century CE (100–199 CE)
centuries.summary: The 2nd century sees Rome at its imperial peak under the 'Five Good Emperors,' the continued spread and diversification of Christianity, fierce theological debates, and the final suppression of Jewish revolt. Gnosticism flourishes alongside proto-orthodox Christianity.

facts.fact: The 'Five Good Emperors' — Nerva, Trajan, Hadrian, Antoninus Pius, and Marcus Aurelius — ruled from 96–180 CE, a period Edward Gibbon called the epoch 'in the history of the world during which the condition of the human race was most happy and prosperous.'

Trajan (98–117 CE) expanded the Roman Empire to its greatest territorial extent, conquering Dacia (modern Romania) and briefly taking Mesopotamia — feats celebrated on Trajan's Column in Rome, which still stands.

Hadrian (117–138 CE) famously pulled back from Trajan's eastern conquests and consolidated the empire's borders. He built Hadrian's Wall across northern Britain — 73 miles long — to mark the empire's northern limit.

The Bar Kokhba Revolt (132–135 CE) was the third and final Jewish revolt against Rome. After its brutal suppression by Hadrian, Jews were banned from Jerusalem, which was rebuilt as the Roman city Aelia Capitolina.

Hadrian's suppression of the Bar Kokhba revolt effectively ended the Jewish homeland for nearly 1,800 years. Ehrman notes this further severed the already strained relationship between Judaism and the growing Christian movement.

Marcus Aurelius (161–180 CE) was both a philosopher-emperor and a military commander. His private journal, 'Meditations,' is a foundational Stoic text — written in Greek as a personal exercise, never intended for publication.

Bart Ehrman's work highlights that in the 2nd century, Christianity was not a unified religion but a sprawling collection of competing sects — including Gnostics, Marcionites, and proto-orthodox groups — each claiming to represent the true teaching of Jesus.

Marcion of Sinope (~85–160 CE) argued that the God of the Old Testament was a different, inferior deity from the loving Father of Jesus. His radical canon — only Paul's letters and a edited Luke — forced the proto-orthodox church to begin defining its own canon.

Gnosticism was a diverse religious movement that held secret spiritual knowledge ('gnosis') as the path to salvation. Gnostic Christians produced their own gospels — including the Gospel of Thomas and the Gospel of Philip — discovered at Nag Hammadi, Egypt in 1945.

The Gospel of Thomas, likely compiled in the 2nd century, contains 114 sayings attributed to Jesus with no narrative framework and no death/resurrection. Some sayings may preserve authentic early traditions; others are distinctly Gnostic.

Polycarp of Smyrna (~69–155 CE), a bishop who claimed to have known the Apostle John, was martyred by Roman authorities. His execution is one of the earliest detailed accounts of Christian martyrdom and became a template for later martyr literature.

Justin Martyr (~100–165 CE) was one of the first Christian 'apologists' — intellectuals who defended Christianity to pagan audiences using Greek philosophy. He was executed in Rome for refusing to sacrifice to Roman gods.

Irenaeus of Lyon (~130–202 CE) wrote 'Against Heresies,' a massive attack on Gnostic Christianity. He championed four Gospels — Matthew, Mark, Luke, John — as the only legitimate ones, a crucial step toward the later canonical New Testament.

Ehrman argues that what we call 'orthodoxy' was not the original form of Christianity but the version that won a power struggle — written by the victors who then branded all competitors as 'heretics.'

The Antonine Plague (165–180 CE) swept through the Roman Empire, killing an estimated 5–10 million people — possibly smallpox. It was brought back by soldiers returning from campaigns in Mesopotamia and significantly weakened the empire.
Ptolemy's 'Almagest,' written around 150 CE, compiled the Greco-Roman world's astronomical knowledge into a geocentric model of the universe that would dominate Western science for over 1,300 years.

Galen of Pergamon (~129–216 CE) became the most influential physician in Roman history, synthesizing Greek medical knowledge. His writings on anatomy and medicine remained authoritative in Europe and the Islamic world until the Renaissance.

The rise of Roman roads — over 250,000 miles by the 2nd century — enabled not just military movement but the rapid spread of ideas, goods, and religions including Christianity and Mithraism across the empire.

Mithraism, a mystery religion popular among Roman soldiers, shared several features with early Christianity: a savior figure, communal meals, baptism, and a focus on life after death. Scholars debate whether Christianity borrowed from Mithraism or both drew on common cultural wells.

By the end of the 2nd century, the New Testament canon was beginning to take shape — but was far from settled. Different churches used different collections of texts, and debates over which books counted as authoritative would continue for centuries.

centuries.label: 3rd Century CE (200–299 CE)
centuries.summary: The 3rd century is Rome's crisis period — a revolving door of emperors, military chaos, plague, economic collapse, and near-disintegration. Meanwhile Christianity grows rapidly, survives brutal persecution, and begins to develop institutional structures.

facts.fact: The 'Crisis of the Third Century' (235–284 CE) saw Rome nearly collapse: over 50 emperors or claimants in 50 years, constant civil war, barbarian invasions on multiple frontiers, and devastating plague.

The Antonine dynasty ended with Commodus (180–192 CE), an erratic emperor who declared himself a god and the reincarnation of Hercules. He was assassinated by his wrestling partner, plunging Rome back into civil war.
Septimius Severus (193–211 CE) won the civil war after 193 CE's 'Year of the Five Emperors' and founded the Severan dynasty. He was born in North Africa — the first African-born Roman emperor.

Caracalla (211–217 CE), son of Severus, issued the Edict of Caracalla in 212 CE, granting Roman citizenship to virtually all free inhabitants of the empire — a massive legal and political transformation.

The Plague of Cyprian (249–262 CE) killed up to 5,000 people per day in Rome at its peak. The Christian response — caring for the sick regardless of faith — contributed significantly to the religion's rapid growth in this era.

Decius (249–251 CE) launched the first empire-wide, systematic persecution of Christians, requiring all citizens to sacrifice to Roman gods and obtain a certificate of compliance. Christians who refused faced imprisonment, torture, or death.

The Decian persecution forced a crisis within Christianity: what to do with 'lapsed' Christians who had sacrificed to avoid execution? The debate over whether they could be readmitted to the church split communities and produced lasting theological controversy.

Origen of Alexandria (~185–253 CE) was the most prolific and intellectually ambitious Christian scholar of the early church. He produced the Hexapla (a six-column comparison of Old Testament versions) and wrote hundreds of theological works, though later declared heretical.

Ehrman highlights Origen as an example of how diverse early Christian thought was — Origen believed in the pre-existence of souls and the eventual salvation of all beings (apokatastasis), positions later condemned as heresy.

The Manichaean religion was founded by the prophet Mani (~216–274 CE) in Persia. A radical dualistic faith blending Zoroastrianism, Buddhism, and Christianity, it spread across the Roman Empire and into China — and would later captivate the young Augustine of Hippo.
Zenobia, Queen of Palmyra, carved out a breakaway empire in the eastern Roman provinces (~270–272 CE), briefly ruling Egypt and much of the Middle East before being defeated by Emperor Aurelian.

Aurelian (270–275 CE) reunified the fragmented Roman Empire after the Crisis of the Third Century — earning the title 'Restorer of the World.' He also built the Aurelian Wall around Rome, still visible today.

Diocletian (284–305 CE) ended the Crisis of the Third Century through radical administrative reform, splitting the empire into four zones ruled by two senior and two junior emperors — the 'Tetrarchy.'

Diocletian launched the 'Great Persecution' (303–312 CE) — the most severe and systematic Roman persecution of Christians. Churches were destroyed, scriptures burned, and Christians stripped of legal rights across the empire.

The concept of 'scripture' itself was contested during this period. When Roman authorities demanded Christians hand over their sacred texts, some complied — earning the label 'traditor' (one who hands over), the root of the English word 'traitor.'

Neoplatonism, developed by Plotinus (~205–270 CE), became the dominant philosophical school of the late Roman world. Its concept of a transcendent, ineffable 'One' as the source of all existence deeply influenced Christian theology.

The Sassanid Persian Empire (founded 224 CE) replaced the Parthian Empire as Rome's great eastern rival, initiating centuries of warfare that would drain both empires and eventually leave them vulnerable to the Arab conquests of the 7th century.

Bart Ehrman notes that the diversity of early Christianity is most visible in the manuscripts of this era — copyists regularly altered texts, whether accidentally or deliberately, to harmonize discrepancies or promote particular theological views.

By the mid-3rd century, the Christian church had developed a clear hierarchical structure: bishops overseeing individual cities, with Rome, Alexandria, and Antioch emerging as the most prestigious sees — foreshadowing the later power of the papacy.

Despite three major empire-wide persecutions by the end of the 3rd century, Christianity had grown from a small Jewish sect to a religion with millions of adherents across the Roman Empire — remarkable growth that historians attribute to community care, apocalyptic appeal, and organizational strength.

centuries.label: 4th Century CE (300–399 CE)
centuries.summary: The 4th century is one of history's great turning points: Constantine converts to Christianity, transforming a persecuted sect into the empire's favored religion. The Council of Nicaea defines orthodoxy, and by century's end Christianity becomes Rome's official religion.

facts.fact: Constantine I defeated his rivals at the Battle of Milvian Bridge (312 CE), allegedly after seeing a vision of a Christian symbol. Whether his conversion was genuine or politically calculated, it permanently changed the Roman Empire.

The Edict of Milan (313 CE), issued by Constantine and his co-emperor Licinius, granted religious tolerance throughout the Roman Empire — ending official persecution of Christians and restoring confiscated church property.

Constantine convened the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE — the first empire-wide Christian council — to resolve the Arian controversy: whether Jesus was co-eternal with God the Father or a created being subordinate to him.

The Council of Nicaea produced the Nicene Creed, declaring the Son 'of the same substance' (homoousios) as the Father — a position that condemned Arianism as heresy. Ehrman notes this was a political as much as theological resolution.

Arianism — the belief that Jesus was divine but subordinate to and created by God the Father — was not immediately defeated by Nicaea. It remained dominant in much of the empire and spread to Germanic tribes, persisting for centuries.

Constantine founded Constantinople (modern Istanbul) in 330 CE on the site of the Greek city Byzantium, dedicating it as the new eastern capital of the empire. It would remain one of the world's great cities for over 1,000 years.

Bart Ehrman emphasizes that Constantine's patronage transformed Christianity institutionally: churches were lavishly funded, clergy received tax exemptions, and Sunday became an official day of rest — changes that permanently shaped Western civilization.

Julian 'the Apostate' (361–363 CE) was the last pagan Roman emperor. A nephew of Constantine who was raised Christian but abandoned the faith, he tried to restore traditional Roman religion — and died on campaign against Persia before he could succeed.

Ambrose, Bishop of Milan (374–397 CE), famously forced Emperor Theodosius I to do public penance for ordering a massacre at Thessalonica — an extraordinary assertion of church authority over imperial power.

Theodosius I issued the Edict of Thessalonica (380 CE), declaring Nicene Christianity the official state religion of the Roman Empire. Paganism was effectively outlawed, and heresy became a crime against the state.

The First Council of Constantinople (381 CE) expanded the Nicene Creed to its current form, settling debates about the Holy Spirit and establishing the Trinity doctrine that defines mainstream Christianity to this day.

The Sack of the Serapeum (391 CE) — destruction of Alexandria's great temple to the god Serapis by a Christian mob, allegedly with imperial approval — symbolized the violent suppression of paganism in the newly Christian empire.

The great Library of Alexandria was not destroyed in a single dramatic event. It declined gradually over centuries; a significant portion may have been damaged during Julius Caesar's visit (48 BCE), with further losses under various rulers including Christian ones.

Athanasius of Alexandria (296–373 CE) was exiled five times for defending Nicene orthodoxy against Arian emperors. In 367 CE, his Easter Letter listed the 27 books of the New Testament — the first known list matching the modern canon exactly.

Jerome (~347–420 CE) began his Latin translation of the Bible — the Vulgate — which would become the authoritative Bible of the Western church for over a thousand years. His translation choices shaped Christian theology in profound ways.

Augustine of Hippo (354–430 CE) was born in North Africa, lived as a Manichaean for nine years, and converted to Christianity in 386 CE under Ambrose's influence. He would become the most influential theologian in Western Christianity.

The Huns, nomadic warriors from the Eurasian steppe, pushed westward under Attila's predecessors, driving Germanic peoples — Visigoths, Ostrogoths, Vandals — into Roman territory and triggering the migrations that would end the Western Empire.

The Battle of Adrianople (378 CE) was a catastrophic Roman defeat by the Visigoths — the first time a Roman emperor (Valens) died in battle against a foreign enemy in centuries. Tom Holland (The Rest is History) describes it as the moment Rome lost control of its frontiers.

Ehrman's 'Misquoting Jesus' focuses heavily on this period — by the 4th century, thousands of manuscript copies of Christian texts existed, and scribal variations (both accidental and deliberate) had multiplied enormously, making the recovery of 'original' texts enormously difficult.

The Codex Sinaiticus, produced around 330–360 CE and discovered at St. Catherine's Monastery on the Sinai Peninsula, is one of the oldest and most complete manuscripts of the Christian Bible — and contains books not in the modern canon alongside some that are.

centuries.label: 5th Century CE (400–499 CE)
centuries.summary: The 5th century witnesses the fall of the Western Roman Empire, the rise of Augustine's theology, the great Christological controversies, and the emergence of a new post-Roman world shaped by Germanic kingdoms and the enduring Eastern Empire.

facts.fact: In 410 CE, the Visigoths under Alaric sacked Rome — the first time the city had been taken by a foreign enemy in 800 years. The shock reverberated across the empire and the Christian world, prompting Augustine to write 'The City of God.'

Augustine's 'The City of God' (413–426 CE) argued that Rome's fall was irrelevant to the true Christian — whose citizenship was in the eternal City of God, not any earthly empire. It became the foundational text of Western Christian political theology.

Augustine's doctrine of original sin — that Adam's sin corrupted all humanity, who are therefore born guilty and deserve damnation without God's grace — was largely his invention, shaped by his debates with the monk Pelagius.

Pelagius (~354–418 CE) argued that humans have free will and can choose good without God's grace — a position condemned as heresy at Augustine's urging. The debate between Augustinian predestination and Pelagian free will echoes through Christianity to the present day.

The Council of Ephesus (431 CE) condemned Nestorius, who argued that Mary should be called 'Christotokos' (bearer of Christ) rather than 'Theotokos' (bearer of God). The council declared Mary 'Mother of God' — a title that elevated Marian devotion enormously.

The Council of Chalcedon (451 CE) declared that Christ has two complete natures — fully divine and fully human — united in one person. This formula was rejected by many Eastern churches (Coptic, Armenian, Ethiopian), causing splits that persist today.

Attila the Hun terrorized the Western Empire in the 440s–450s CE, extracting enormous tribute and raiding deep into Gaul and Italy. Pope Leo I's negotiation with Attila in 452 CE, convincing him to spare Rome, became legendary.

The Western Roman Empire's final collapse is conventionally dated to 476 CE, when the Germanic chieftain Odoacer deposed Romulus Augustulus, the last Western emperor, and sent the imperial regalia to Constantinople.

Tom Holland (The Rest is History) argues that 476 CE was less a dramatic collapse than a gradual transformation — many Romans in the West continued daily life under Germanic kings who largely preserved Roman administrative structures.

The Vandals, a Germanic people, crossed into North Africa in 429 CE and established a kingdom there. In 455 CE they sacked Rome — giving their name permanently to the English language as a byword for mindless destruction.

Patrick (~387–461 CE) was taken as a slave to Ireland as a teenager, escaped, and returned as a missionary bishop, converting much of the island to Christianity. Ireland would become a crucial center of learning preserving classical texts through the 'Dark Ages.'

The monastic movement expanded dramatically in the 5th century. John Cassian (~360–435 CE) brought Eastern desert monasticism to the West, and his writings on the spiritual life shaped Benedictine monasticism and Western Christianity.

Proclus (~412–485 CE), the last great Neoplatonist philosopher in Athens, synthesized centuries of pagan philosophy into a comprehensive system. His work profoundly influenced Islamic, Jewish, and Christian mysticism.

The Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire not only survived the 5th century but thrived. Emperor Theodosius II (408–450 CE) built the massive Theodosian Walls around Constantinople — walls that would protect the city for a thousand years.

The Theodosian Code (438 CE) compiled all Roman imperial legislation since Constantine — a massive legal project that preserved Roman law and influenced the legal systems of medieval Europe and, through them, the modern Western world.

Clovis I (~466–511 CE), king of the Franks, converted to Catholic Christianity (rather than Arian) around 496 CE — a conversion with enormous long-term consequences, as it aligned the most powerful Germanic kingdom with Rome and set the stage for medieval Christendom.

Boethius (~477–524 CE), a Roman senator serving the Ostrogothic king Theodoric, wrote 'The Consolation of Philosophy' while awaiting execution. It became one of the most widely read books in the Middle Ages — notably, it makes almost no mention of Christianity.

Ehrman points out that the 5th century saw the effective closure of the New Testament canon in the West — though Eastern churches continued to dispute several books (Revelation, 2 Peter, 2–3 John) well into subsequent centuries.

The great Alexandrian philosopher Hypatia (born ~360 CE) was murdered by a Christian mob in 415 CE. She has become an iconic symbol of the conflict between classical learning and religious fanaticism — though the reality was more politically complex.

By 500 CE, the Mediterranean world had been fundamentally transformed: the unified Roman Empire was gone, replaced by a Latin Christian West fragmented among Germanic kingdoms, a Greek Christian Byzantine East, and a Persian Sassanid Empire — a tripartite world that set the stage for the rise of Islam.

centuries.label: 6th Century CE (500–599 CE)
centuries.summary: The 6th century sees the final dissolution of the Western Roman order, the rise of Justinian's Byzantine Empire, the birth of Islam's precursor conditions, the spread of monasticism, and the devastating Plague of Justinian — the first great pandemic of the medieval world.

facts.fact: Justinian I (527–565 CE) was the most ambitious Byzantine emperor, reconquering North Africa, Italy, and parts of Spain from Germanic kingdoms in a bold attempt to restore the Roman Empire.
The Hagia Sophia, built by Justinian in Constantinople between 532–537 CE, was the largest cathedral in the world for nearly a thousand years — an architectural marvel that still stands today.

The Plague of Justinian (541–549 CE) was the first pandemic of bubonic plague in recorded history, killing an estimated 25–50 million people and devastating the Byzantine Empire's economy and military.

The Justinian Code (Corpus Juris Civilis, 529–534 CE) compiled and rationalized Roman law into a single authoritative text — the foundation of legal systems across Europe and Latin America to this day.

Benedict of Nursia (~480–547 CE) wrote the Benedictine Rule, establishing the framework for Western monasticism: communal life, regular prayer, manual labor, and stability. Benedictine monasteries would become the cultural backbone of medieval Europe.

The Nika Riots (532 CE) nearly toppled Justinian — Constantinople's chariot-racing factions united against him, burning half the city. His empress Theodora famously shamed him out of fleeing, and the rebellion was crushed with 30,000 deaths.
Theodora (~500–548 CE), Justinian's empress, was one of the most powerful women in Byzantine history — a former actress who became co-ruler, influencing theology, law, and foreign policy.

The Ostrogothic Kingdom of Italy, established by Theodoric the Great, collapsed under Byzantine pressure during the Gothic Wars (535–554 CE), devastating Italy's population and infrastructure far more than the 476 CE 'fall' ever had.
Gregory of Tours (~538–594 CE) wrote the 'History of the Franks,' our primary source for early medieval Gaul — a world of Frankish kings, Gallo-Roman bishops, and miraculous saints.
The prophet Muhammad was born around 570 CE in Mecca — a city in the Arabian Peninsula that was then a significant trading hub between Byzantine and Sassanid spheres of influence.

Boethius's 'Consolation of Philosophy,' written while awaiting execution around 524 CE, became one of the most copied books of the Middle Ages, shaping how medieval thinkers approached fate, providence, and the nature of good.

The conversion of the Visigoths from Arianism to Catholic Christianity under King Reccared (589 CE) unified Iberian Christianity under Rome — a precedent for the close alliance of church and Frankish kingdom that defined medieval Christendom.

Pope Gregory I ('the Great', 590–604 CE) transformed the papacy into an administrative power, sending missionaries to England, corresponding with kings across Europe, and reforming church music — Gregorian chant bears his name.

The Sassanid Persian Empire reached its greatest extent under Khosrow I (531–579 CE), defeating Byzantine armies and establishing Persia as a major cultural center — its court at Ctesiphon attracted scholars, philosophers, and physicians from across the known world.

Irish monasticism, pioneered by figures like Columba (~521–597 CE), produced the most vibrant intellectual culture in the post-Roman West — Irish monks preserved Latin learning and sent missionaries back to a Christianizing Europe.

The Avars, a nomadic people from the Eurasian steppe, arrived in Europe around 560 CE and established a powerful khanate in the Pannonian Basin (modern Hungary), destabilizing the Balkans for two centuries.

Silk production, long a Chinese monopoly, was smuggled to the Byzantine Empire around 552 CE — reportedly by monks who hid silkworm eggs in hollow canes — breaking China's stranglehold on the luxury trade.

The Council of Constantinople III (553 CE), convened by Justinian, condemned the 'Three Chapters' — writings associated with Nestorian Christianity — in an attempt to reconcile Eastern and Western church factions that instead deepened divisions.

Augustine of Canterbury, sent by Pope Gregory I, arrived in England in 597 CE and baptized King Æthelberht of Kent — beginning the Roman Christian mission to the Anglo-Saxons that would eventually produce the English church.

The 6th century saw the final collapse of urban life across most of the former Western Roman Empire — archaeological evidence shows dramatic population decline, abandonment of cities, and the near-disappearance of long-distance trade in the Latin West.

centuries.label: 7th Century CE (600–699 CE)
centuries.summary: The 7th century is one of history's most transformative — the birth of Islam, the Arab conquests that reshaped the entire Middle East and North Africa within decades, and the permanent weakening of both Byzantium and Persia that set the stage for the medieval world.

facts.fact: Muhammad received his first revelation around 610 CE in a cave outside Mecca, beginning the process that would produce the Quran and the religion of Islam — within a century, Islam would be the dominant faith from Spain to Central Asia.
The Hijra (622 CE) — Muhammad's migration from Mecca to Medina — marks the beginning of the Islamic calendar and the founding of the first Muslim community-state, the umma.

Muhammad died in 632 CE without clearly designating a successor, immediately triggering the succession crisis that produced the Sunni-Shia split — one of the most consequential religious divisions in history.

The Battle of Yarmouk (636 CE) saw Arab Muslim forces decisively defeat the Byzantine army, permanently ending Roman control of Syria, Palestine, and Egypt — territories that had been Roman for 700 years.

The Arab conquest of Persia (633–651 CE) ended the Sassanid Empire entirely — one of the ancient world's great powers collapsed within two decades of Muhammad's death, absorbed into the expanding Islamic caliphate.

Jerusalem fell to Arab forces under Caliph Umar in 637 CE. Unlike the Roman destructions, Umar's conquest was notably peaceful — he guaranteed the safety of Christian and Jewish inhabitants and visited the Church of the Holy Sepulchre without praying inside, to prevent it being claimed as a mosque.

The assassination of Caliph Uthman (656 CE) and the subsequent First Fitna (Islamic Civil War) established the Sunni-Shia divide: supporters of Ali (Muhammad's cousin and son-in-law) became Shia Islam; those who accepted Muawiya's caliphate became Sunni.

The Battle of Karbala (680 CE) — in which Hussein, grandson of Muhammad, was killed with 72 companions by Umayyad forces — became the defining martyr narrative of Shia Islam, commemorated annually in Ashura.

The Umayyad Caliphate, established in Damascus after 661 CE, built the first great Islamic empire — stretching from Spain to Central Asia — and commissioned the Dome of the Rock in Jerusalem (691 CE), one of Islam's holiest sites.

The Dome of the Rock, completed 691 CE, was built over the rock from which Muslims believe Muhammad ascended to heaven on the Night Journey — it is also the site of the Jewish Temple, making it one of the most contested sacred sites in the world.

The Arab conquest of Egypt (639–642 CE) ended 700 years of Greco-Roman rule and began the Arabization and Islamization of North Africa — transforming the region's language, culture, and religion over the following centuries.

The Quran was compiled into a standardized written text under Caliph Uthman (~650 CE) — before this, it existed primarily in oral form and in scattered written fragments. The 'Uthmanic codex' remains the standard text used today.

Tom Holland's 'Dominion' argues that the Islamic conquest fundamentally challenged Christianity's claim to universal truth — the rapid success of Islam forced Christian theologians to rethink the relationship between faith, history, and divine providence.

The Battle of Tours/Poitiers (732 CE — technically 8th century) halted Arab expansion into Western Europe, but by 700 CE Arab forces had already crossed into Spain (711 CE begins) — the Iberian Peninsula would be partially Islamic for nearly 800 years.

The Byzantine-Arab Wars of the 7th century permanently reduced Byzantium to a rump state centered on Anatolia and the Balkans — it survived, but never recovered its former breadth or military strength.

The Lombards invaded Italy in 568 CE and controlled much of the peninsula by 600 CE, creating a fragmented Italy of competing Lombard, Byzantine, and papal territories that would persist until the 19th century.

Heraclius (610–641 CE) was the Byzantine emperor who defeated Persia in a near-miraculous comeback (627 CE), only to see his life's work immediately swept away by the Arab conquests — history's cruellest reversal of fortune for a victorious general.

The Synod of Whitby (664 CE) settled the conflict between Roman and Celtic Christianity in England, choosing Rome's dating of Easter over the Irish tradition — aligning England with continental Christendom.

Mu'awiya I, founder of the Umayyad dynasty, transformed the Islamic caliphate from a tribal Arab confederation into a sophisticated imperial administration — borrowing Byzantine and Persian bureaucratic techniques.

By 700 CE, Arabic was becoming the administrative language of a vast empire — a linguistic unification that accelerated the translation of Greek scientific and philosophical texts into Arabic, preserving and transmitting ancient knowledge to both the Islamic world and, eventually, medieval Europe.

centuries.label: 8th Century CE (700–799 CE)
centuries.summary: The 8th century sees Islam's expansion reach its limits, Charlemagne forge a new Christian empire in the West, and the Islamic Golden Age begin its extraordinary flowering of science, philosophy, and medicine that would preserve and transmit ancient knowledge.

facts.fact: The Arab conquest of the Iberian Peninsula began in 711 CE when Tariq ibn Ziyad crossed from North Africa — within seven years, most of Spain and Portugal was under Muslim rule, beginning the period known as Al-Andalus.

The Battle of Tours/Poitiers (732 CE) halted the Arab advance into Francia — Charles Martel's Frankish forces defeated an Umayyad raiding force. Tom Holland notes it was less civilization-defining than later legend claimed, but did mark the effective northern limit of Islamic expansion in Europe.

The Abbasid Revolution (750 CE) overthrew the Umayyad Caliphate, shifting Islam's center of gravity from Damascus to Baghdad — the new dynasty was more cosmopolitan, incorporating Persian administrative traditions and opening the Islamic world to broader intellectual exchange.

Baghdad was founded in 762 CE by Caliph Al-Mansur as the Abbasid capital — within a generation it became the world's largest city, a cosmopolitan hub of trade, scholarship, and culture with over a million inhabitants.

The House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) in Baghdad, established in the late 8th century, became the center of the Islamic Golden Age — scholars translated Greek, Persian, and Indian texts into Arabic, preserving Aristotle, Euclid, and Galen for future generations.

Charlemagne (768–814 CE) united most of Western Europe under Frankish rule through decades of military campaigns — against the Saxons, Lombards, Avars, and Spanish Muslims — creating the largest Western European state since Rome.

On Christmas Day, 800 CE, Pope Leo III crowned Charlemagne 'Emperor of the Romans' in St. Peter's Basilica — a deliberate assertion that Western Christendom had its own emperor, independent of (and rival to) the Byzantine emperor in Constantinople.

The Iconoclasm Controversy (726–843 CE) split the Byzantine Empire and widened the divide between Eastern and Western Christianity — Emperor Leo III ordered the destruction of religious images; the papacy and most of the West rejected this, deepening the rift that would produce the Great Schism.

The Viking Age began around 793 CE with the raid on Lindisfarne monastery in northeastern England — inaugurating two centuries of Norse expansion, raiding, trading, and settlement that reshaped northern Europe.

Al-Andalus (Muslim Spain) under the Umayyad emirate became one of Europe's most sophisticated civilizations — Córdoba rivaled Constantinople and Baghdad in population, libraries, and intellectual culture, hosting Jewish, Christian, and Muslim scholars together.

The Carolingian Renaissance under Charlemagne revived literacy, scholarship, and standardized Latin script across Western Europe — the minuscule script developed at Charlemagne's court is the direct ancestor of modern lowercase letters.

Charles Martel's victory at Tours led to a Frankish military revolution — the rise of heavily armored cavalry (knights) financed by grants of land (the origin of feudalism) that would define European warfare and society for centuries.

The Tang Dynasty of China (618–907 CE) reached its golden age in the 8th century under Emperor Xuanzong — a cosmopolitan empire of 80 million people, China was simultaneously the world's most advanced civilization in poetry, painting, and ceramic arts.

The Battle of Talas River (751 CE) pitted Arab Abbasid forces against the Tang Chinese army in Central Asia — the Arabs won, but more significantly, Chinese papermaking technology spread westward through captured craftsmen, eventually transforming Islamic and European learning.

The Umayyad emirate of Córdoba (756 CE) was established by Abd al-Rahman I, the sole Umayyad survivor of the Abbasid massacre — creating an independent Muslim Spain that refused to recognize the Abbasid caliphate in Baghdad.

Offa of Mercia (757–796 CE), the most powerful Anglo-Saxon king before Alfred the Great, built Offa's Dyke — an 80-mile earthwork barrier along the Welsh border still visible today — and standardized English coinage.

The Khazar Khaganate, a Turkic empire dominating the steppes north of the Caucasus, converted to Judaism in the 8th century — one of history's most unusual religious conversions, creating a Jewish state between Christian Byzantium and Islamic Persia.

Empress Wu Zetian (690–705 CE), the only woman to rule China in her own name, expanded the Tang empire, reformed the civil service examination, and patronized Buddhism — her reign remains deeply controversial in Chinese historiography.

The development of Arabic numerals (originally Indian) in the 8th century Islamic world — and their eventual transmission to Europe via Al-Andalus — was one of the most consequential mathematical transfers in history, making modern arithmetic and algebra possible.

By 800 CE, the medieval world had taken its essential shape: a fragmented Latin Christian West under Charlemagne, a shrinking but resilient Byzantine East, and a vast, sophisticated Islamic civilization stretching from Spain to Central Asia — three civilizations that would interact, conflict, and borrow from each other for centuries.

centuries.label: 9th Century CE (800–899 CE)
centuries.summary: The 9th century sees Charlemagne's empire fragment, the Vikings reshape northern Europe, Alfred the Great defend England, and the Islamic Golden Age produce its greatest scientists and philosophers — while Byzantine missionaries bring Christianity and literacy to the Slavic world.

facts.fact: Charlemagne died in 814 CE and his empire was divided among his grandsons by the Treaty of Verdun (843 CE) into three kingdoms — West Francia (France), East Francia (Germany), and Middle Francia — the political geography of modern Europe begins here.

Alfred the Great (871–899 CE) defended England against the Great Heathen Army of Vikings, negotiated the Danelaw (Viking-controlled northern England), and launched a remarkable cultural revival — translating Latin texts into Old English and founding schools.

The Great Heathen Army (865 CE) was a massive Viking force that conquered most of Anglo-Saxon England within a decade — only Alfred's Wessex survived, making him the only English king to earn the title 'Great.'

The Rus' people — Scandinavian traders and warriors who gave their name to Russia — established the city of Novgorod around 862 CE and founded the Rurik dynasty, the origin state of what became Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus.

Cyril and Methodius, Byzantine missionaries sent to the Slavic peoples in 863 CE, created the Glagolitic alphabet (ancestor of Cyrillic) and translated the Bible into Old Church Slavonic — bringing literacy and Orthodox Christianity to Eastern Europe.

Al-Khwarizmi (~780–850 CE) wrote 'Al-Kitab al-mukhtasar fi hisab al-jabr' in Baghdad — the book that gave us the word 'algebra.' His name also gives us 'algorithm.' His work synthesized Greek and Indian mathematics into a new system.

The philosopher Al-Kindi (~801–873 CE) was the first great Islamic philosopher to systematically reconcile Greek philosophy with Islamic theology — his work on optics, cryptography, and music theory was centuries ahead of the European West.
The Viking settlement of Iceland began around 874 CE — establishing what would become the world's oldest surviving parliament (the Althing, 930 CE) and a literary culture that produced the sagas.

The Carolingian Empire's fragmentation produced the feudal system in its mature form — local lords providing military service to regional counts, who served kings, in exchange for land grants (fiefs). The inability of kings to project central power made local strongmen essential.

The siege of Paris by Vikings (885–886 CE) demonstrated both the Vikings' reach and the Carolingian dynasty's weakness — King Charles the Fat paid the Vikings to leave rather than fight, and was deposed the following year.

Emperor Basil I (867–886 CE) founded the Macedonian dynasty, reviving Byzantine power — his successors would preside over a Byzantine cultural golden age and military expansion that temporarily recovered much of the territory lost to the Arabs.

The Aghlabid conquest of Sicily from Byzantium (827–902 CE) created a sophisticated Islamic emirate that became one of the Mediterranean's most prosperous and cultured societies, blending Arab, Byzantine, and Norman influences.

Ibn Ishaq's biography of Muhammad, compiled in the 8th century and reworked by Ibn Hisham in the 9th, became the standard life of the Prophet — shaping how Muslims understood Islamic origins for centuries.

The 9th-century Islamic scholar Al-Battani produced astronomical tables of extraordinary accuracy, correcting Ptolemy's calculations and determining the solar year's length to within seconds — his work was used by Copernicus 700 years later.

The Great Schism between Rome and Constantinople was prefigured by the Photian Schism (863–867 CE) — a conflict over the appointment of Patriarch Photius that exposed the deepening theological and political divide between Eastern and Western Christianity.

The Abbasid Caliphate began its political decline in the 9th century as Turkish slave-soldiers (ghulam/mamluks) became the real power behind the throne — the caliph in Baghdad increasingly a figurehead while Turkish commanders controlled the army.

The Kingdom of Asturias in northern Spain, the Christian remnant after the Muslim conquest, began the Reconquista — the slow, centuries-long effort to reclaim the Iberian Peninsula for Christianity, finally completed in 1492.

Photius of Constantinople (~810–893 CE), one of the most learned men of the medieval world, compiled the 'Bibliotheca' — summaries of 280 books, many now lost, preserving the titles and contents of classical works that would otherwise be unknown.

The Carolingian minuscule script, standardized under Charlemagne and refined in the 9th century, became the basis for modern lowercase letters — and was revived by Renaissance humanists who mistook it for classical Roman script.

By 900 CE, the outlines of medieval Europe were clear: a patchwork of feudal kingdoms under the Latin church in the West, an Orthodox Byzantine civilization in the East, a sophisticated Islamic world from Spain to Central Asia, and a vast Viking diaspora reshaping the North Atlantic.

centuries.label: 10th Century CE (900–999 CE)
centuries.summary: The 10th century sees Otto I forge the Holy Roman Empire, the Vikings discover America, the Song Dynasty revive Chinese civilization, and the Byzantine Empire reach its medieval peak — while the Islamic world begins to fragment and the Slavic kingdoms of Eastern Europe take shape.

facts.fact: Otto I of Germany (936–973 CE) defeated the Magyars at the Battle of Lechfeld (955 CE), was crowned Holy Roman Emperor by Pope John XII in 962 CE, and established the political framework that would dominate Central Europe for nearly a thousand years.

The Holy Roman Empire — neither holy, nor Roman, nor an empire (as Voltaire later quipped) — was a loose confederation of German, Italian, and Central European territories under an elected emperor whose authority was constantly contested by princes and popes.

Leif Eriksson reached North America around 1000 CE (technically just after the century's end), establishing a settlement at L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland — the first confirmed European contact with the Americas, 500 years before Columbus.

The Christianization of Scandinavia accelerated in the 10th century — Harald Bluetooth of Denmark converted around 960 CE, beginning the process that transformed Viking raiders into Christian kingdoms integrated into European civilization.

The Song Dynasty (960–1279 CE) reunified China after decades of fragmentation and inaugurated one of the most commercially and intellectually sophisticated civilizations in world history — printing, paper money, the compass, and gunpowder all developed or spread in this era.

Al-Andalus under Abd al-Rahman III (912–961 CE) reached its golden age — Córdoba became Europe's largest city, with a library of 400,000 volumes, public baths, and street lighting, while most of Europe lived in relative poverty and illiteracy.

The Byzantine Empire under Basil II 'the Bulgar-Slayer' (976–1025 CE) reached its medieval peak — reconquering Bulgaria, Armenia, and parts of Syria, and cementing Byzantine cultural dominance over Orthodox Eastern Europe.

Vladimir I of Kiev (980–1015 CE) converted to Orthodox Christianity in 988 CE and forcibly baptized his subjects in the Dnieper River — the Christianization of Rus' that made Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus permanently Orthodox.

The Magyar invasions of the 9th–10th centuries terrorized Europe, raiding deep into France, Italy, and the Balkans — until their defeat at Lechfeld (955 CE) forced their settlement in Hungary and eventual Christianization under Stephen I (1000 CE).

Gerbert of Aurillac (~946–1003 CE), who became Pope Sylvester II, introduced Arabic numerals and the abacus to Western Europe — he was so learned that contemporaries suspected him of making a pact with the devil.

The Fatimid Caliphate, established in North Africa in 909 CE and conquering Egypt in 969 CE, built Cairo as its capital and established Al-Azhar University — still one of the world's great centers of Islamic learning.

The Buyid dynasty of Persia captured Baghdad in 945 CE and effectively ended Abbasid political power — the Abbasid caliph became a purely religious figurehead while real power lay with Persian and Turkish military dynasties.

Ibn al-Haytham (Alhazen, ~965–1040 CE), working in Cairo, wrote the 'Book of Optics' — the most important work on vision and light before Newton, establishing the experimental method of scientific investigation centuries before the European scientific revolution.

The development of feudalism in its classic form across 10th-century Europe created a world of castles, knights, serfs, and manors — a response to the inability of weak kings to provide security against Vikings, Magyars, and Saracen raiders.

The Cluny Reform movement, originating at the monastery of Cluny in Burgundy (founded 910 CE), sought to free monasteries from lay control and reform the church — the beginning of a religious reform movement that would eventually produce the papal revolution of the 11th century.
The Iceland Althing, established around 930 CE, was the world's first parliament — a general assembly of free men that made law and settled disputes, preserved in the Icelandic sagas.

The Kingdom of Poland was established by Mieszko I (~960–992 CE), who converted to Christianity in 966 CE — tying Poland permanently to the Latin West and beginning a nation that would play a pivotal role in European history.

The collapse of the Abbasid center gave rise to regional Islamic dynasties — the Samanids in Persia, the Hamdanids in Syria, the Ikhshidids in Egypt — each cultivating Persian or Arabic literary culture independently, producing a golden age of Islamic poetry and philosophy.

Rhazes (Al-Razi, ~854–925 CE), the greatest physician of the medieval Islamic world, wrote 'Al-Hawi' — a 25-volume medical encyclopedia that remained a standard European medical text until the 17th century. He was the first to distinguish smallpox from measles.

By 1000 CE, the world's population had recovered to roughly 300 million — still below its Roman peak. The world was divided among Chinese, Islamic, Byzantine, and Latin Christian civilizations, with a vast zone of steppe nomads connecting them all.

centuries.label: 11th Century CE (1000–1099 CE)
centuries.summary: The 11th century brings the Great Schism between Rome and Constantinople, the Norman Conquest of England, and the First Crusade — three events that permanently shaped the religious and political geography of Europe and the Middle East.

facts.fact: The Great Schism of 1054 CE formally divided Christianity into Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox branches — Pope Leo IX and Patriarch Michael Cerularius excommunicated each other over disputes about papal authority, the filioque clause, and clerical celibacy.

The Norman Conquest of England (1066 CE) — William the Conqueror's victory at the Battle of Hastings — was one of the most consequential events in English history, replacing the Anglo-Saxon elite with a French-speaking Norman aristocracy and transforming the English language.

The Domesday Book (1086 CE), commissioned by William the Conqueror, was a comprehensive survey of England's landholdings and resources — one of the most remarkable administrative achievements of the medieval world and an invaluable historical record.

The First Crusade (1096–1099 CE) was called by Pope Urban II in 1095 CE — a military expedition to recapture Jerusalem from Muslim rule that succeeded beyond all expectation, capturing Jerusalem in 1099 CE after enormous violence.

The Crusaders' capture of Jerusalem in July 1099 CE was followed by a massacre of Muslim and Jewish inhabitants that shocked even contemporaries — Tom Holland notes it as a moment when the Christian ideal of holy war collided catastrophically with its human consequences.

The Battle of Manzikert (1071 CE) was a catastrophic Byzantine defeat by the Seljuk Turks — the emperor Romanos IV was captured, Anatolia was lost, and the Byzantine Empire entered a terminal decline from which it never fully recovered.

The Seljuk Turks, a Turkic dynasty that converted to Sunni Islam, conquered Baghdad in 1055 CE and took control of the Abbasid caliphate — revitalizing Sunni Islam and eventually pushing the Byzantines out of Anatolia.

Anselm of Canterbury (~1033–1109 CE) developed the ontological argument for God's existence — the idea that God, as the greatest conceivable being, must exist because existence is a perfection. It remains one of philosophy's most debated arguments.

The Investiture Controversy (1076–1122 CE) pitted Pope Gregory VII against Holy Roman Emperor Henry IV over the right to appoint (invest) bishops — Henry IV was famously forced to stand barefoot in snow at Canossa to beg papal forgiveness.

Pope Gregory VII's Dictatus Papae (1075 CE) asserted sweeping papal supremacy — the pope could depose emperors, his legates outranked all bishops, and only the pope could call universal councils. It was the most radical assertion of papal power in history.

The scholar Avicenna (Ibn Sina, 980–1037 CE) wrote the 'Canon of Medicine' — a systematic medical encyclopedia that became the standard medical textbook in both Islamic and European universities for 600 years.

The Song Dynasty's printing revolution — using movable type invented by Bi Sheng around 1040 CE — produced books on a scale previously impossible, accelerating literacy and intellectual exchange across China four centuries before Gutenberg.

The Kingdom of Hungary under Stephen I (1000–1038 CE) became a Christian kingdom integrated into European civilization — its coronation crown, sent by Pope Sylvester II, remains a potent symbol of Hungarian national identity.

El Cid (Rodrigo Díaz de Vivar, ~1043–1099 CE) was a Castilian knight who fought for both Christian and Muslim rulers in Spain — his career exemplifies the complex, fluid religious boundaries of Reconquista-era Iberia, far from the simple clash of civilizations it is often portrayed as.

The Viking settlement of Greenland, established by Erik the Red around 985 CE, was still flourishing in the 11th century — a reminder that the Norse world extended across the North Atlantic at a time when most Europeans never left their village.

Omar Khayyam (~1048–1131 CE), the Persian mathematician and poet, solved cubic equations, reformed the Islamic calendar to extraordinary accuracy, and wrote the Rubaiyat — verses on wine, fate, and mortality that became famous in the West through Edward FitzGerald's 19th-century translation.

The founding of Bologna University around 1088 CE — the world's oldest university still in operation — reflected a growing demand for legal and theological education in a more complex European society.

The Almoravid dynasty, Berber reformers from the Sahara, invaded Al-Andalus in 1086 CE at the invitation of Muslim rulers threatened by the Christian Reconquista — replacing the sophisticated, pluralistic Umayyad culture with a stricter religious orthodoxy.

The Norman Kingdom of Sicily, established in the late 11th century, became one of medieval Europe's most remarkable states — a multicultural society where Arabic, Greek, and Latin coexisted under Norman kings who patronized scholars of all three traditions.

By 1100 CE, the Crusades had introduced Western Europeans to the vastly more sophisticated material and intellectual culture of the Islamic world — spices, silk, mathematics, philosophy, and medical knowledge that would fuel the Renaissance centuries later.

centuries.label: 12th Century CE (1100–1199 CE)
centuries.summary: The 12th century is the height of medieval civilization: the Crusader states in the Holy Land, the rise of Gothic architecture, the founding of universities, the rediscovery of Aristotle through Arabic translations, and the stories of Eleanor of Aquitaine and Thomas Becket.

facts.fact: Eleanor of Aquitaine (~1122–1204 CE) was the most powerful woman in 12th-century Europe — queen of France, then queen of England, participant in the Second Crusade, and mother of Richard the Lionheart and King John. Her courts were centers of troubadour culture.

Thomas Becket, Archbishop of Canterbury, was murdered in his own cathedral in 1170 CE by knights acting on the wishes of King Henry II — his martyrdom made Canterbury the greatest pilgrimage site in England and forced Henry into public penance.

The Second Crusade (1147–1149 CE), inspired partly by Bernard of Clairvaux's preaching, was a catastrophic failure — the crusaders besieged Damascus, a Muslim city that had been their ally, and retreated in humiliation.

Saladin (Salah ad-Din, 1137–1193 CE), the Kurdish Muslim leader who unified Egypt and Syria, recaptured Jerusalem in 1187 CE — treating its inhabitants far more humanely than the crusaders had in 1099, in a contrast noted by contemporaries on both sides.

The Third Crusade (1189–1192 CE), led by Richard I of England, Philip II of France, and Frederick Barbarossa of Germany, failed to recapture Jerusalem but negotiated Christian pilgrimage access — Richard and Saladin reached a grudging mutual respect across religious lines.

The founding of the Knights Templar (1119 CE) and Knights Hospitaller created the first military-religious orders — warrior monks who combined the ideals of crusading and monasticism, becoming major military and financial powers in medieval Europe.

Peter Abelard (~1079–1142 CE), the brilliant and controversial philosopher and theologian, pioneered scholasticism — the use of logical analysis to resolve theological disputes. His love affair with Héloïse and subsequent castration by her uncle is one of history's most famous tragedies.

The 12th-century Renaissance saw European scholars travel to Toledo, Palermo, and Antioch to translate Arabic texts of Aristotle, Euclid, and Ptolemy into Latin — triggering an intellectual revolution that transformed European philosophy and science.

Gothic architecture emerged in the Île-de-France around 1140 CE — pointed arches, ribbed vaults, and flying buttresses allowed unprecedented height and light in stone buildings. Notre-Dame de Paris, begun in 1163 CE, is its greatest monument.

Hildegard of Bingen (1098–1179 CE) was a German abbess, composer, theologian, and natural philosopher — one of the most remarkable figures of the Middle Ages, whose mystical visions, medical writings, and musical compositions are still performed today.

The University of Oxford was established in the late 12th century (exact date disputed), joining Bologna and Paris as centers of European higher learning — the university model of organized, degree-granting scholarly communities was one of the Middle Ages' great institutional innovations.

Frederick Barbarossa (1122–1190 CE), Holy Roman Emperor, dominated 12th-century European politics — his conflicts with Pope Alexander III and Italian city-states defined the centuries-long struggle between imperial and papal authority.

The Almohad dynasty replaced the Almoravids in North Africa and Al-Andalus — their strict religious orthodoxy drove Jewish and Christian scholars (including Maimonides) into exile, ending the multicultural golden age of Islamic Spain.

Maimonides (Moses ben Maimon, 1138–1204 CE), the greatest medieval Jewish philosopher, fled Almohad persecution from Córdoba to Cairo, where he wrote the 'Guide for the Perplexed' — reconciling Aristotelian philosophy with Jewish theology, influencing Christian scholasticism.

The Magna Carta's antecedents developed in the 12th century — Henry I's Coronation Charter (1100 CE) made early promises to limit royal arbitrary power that would eventually lead to Magna Carta in 1215.

The Troubadour tradition of southern France created the concept of courtly love — a new poetic and musical culture celebrating romantic love, female virtue, and refined manners that transformed European literature and eventually produced Dante, Petrarch, and Shakespeare.

The Song Dynasty's commercial revolution created the world's first paper money, sophisticated credit systems, and urban markets — China was by far the world's most economically advanced society in the 12th century.

Averroës (Ibn Rushd, 1126–1198 CE), the great Muslim philosopher of Córdoba, wrote the definitive commentaries on Aristotle — transmitted to Europe in Latin translation, they sparked the scholastic movement and deeply influenced Aquinas.
The Byzantine Empire under the Komnenian dynasty experienced a cultural and military revival — but the Fourth Crusade looming in 1204 CE would deliver a blow from which it never recovered.

By 1200 CE, Europe had built over 80 cathedrals and thousands of churches in the Gothic style — a building boom unprecedented since ancient Rome, representing an extraordinary concentration of resources, skill, and faith.

centuries.label: 13th Century CE (1200–1299 CE)
centuries.summary: The 13th century is both the apex and the beginning of the end of medieval civilization: the Mongol conquests reshape Eurasia, the Fourth Crusade sacks Constantinople, Thomas Aquinas synthesizes faith and reason, and Marco Polo opens Europe's eyes to China.

facts.fact: Genghis Khan (1162–1227 CE) united the Mongol tribes and launched the greatest land empire in history — by 1227 CE, the Mongols controlled more territory than any previous empire, and by 1279 CE their descendants ruled from Korea to Poland to Persia.

The Mongol conquests were the most destructive military campaigns in pre-modern history — the sack of Baghdad in 1258 CE ended the Abbasid Caliphate and destroyed the House of Wisdom, killing hundreds of thousands and ending the Islamic Golden Age's intellectual center.

The Fourth Crusade (1202–1204 CE) never reached the Holy Land — diverted by Venetian financial interests, it sacked the Christian city of Constantinople in 1204 CE, establishing a Latin Empire and permanently mortally wounding the Byzantine state.

Magna Carta (1215 CE), forced on King John by rebellious English barons, established for the first time that the king was subject to the rule of law — its clauses protecting due process became the foundation of English constitutional law and eventually influenced the US Constitution.

Thomas Aquinas (1225–1274 CE) wrote the 'Summa Theologica,' the greatest systematic synthesis of Christian theology and Aristotelian philosophy — his work defined Catholic intellectual tradition for centuries and earned him the title 'Doctor Angelicus.'

Francis of Assisi (1181–1226 CE) founded the Franciscan order — a radical embrace of apostolic poverty, care for the poor, and love of nature that offered a different model of Christian life from the wealthy established church. His 'Canticle of the Sun' is the first great poem in the Italian vernacular.

The Albigensian Crusade (1209–1229 CE) was called by Pope Innocent III against the Cathar heretics of southern France — the first crusade against fellow Christians, marked by mass atrocities and the famous command 'Kill them all, God will know his own.'

The Mongol invasion of Europe (1241 CE) devastated Poland and Hungary — the Mongols defeated every European army they faced, and only the death of Khan Ögedei halted the westward advance. Had they continued, European history would have been unrecognizable.

Marco Polo traveled to China (1271–1295 CE) and spent 17 years at the court of Kublai Khan — his account of China's wealth, sophistication, and size astonished Europeans and inspired later explorers including Columbus, who carried a copy of his book.

The Inquisition, formalized by Pope Gregory IX in 1231 CE, established a permanent ecclesiastical court to investigate and prosecute heresy — using torture (authorized by Pope Innocent IV in 1252 CE) to extract confessions.

Roger Bacon (~1214–1294 CE), the English Franciscan friar, advocated for experimental science and predicted flying machines, motorized ships, and microscopes — centuries ahead of his time, his work blended genuine insight with medieval mysticism.

The Battle of Ain Jalut (1260 CE) was the first decisive Mongol defeat — Mamluk forces from Egypt stopped the Mongol advance into the Middle East, saving Egypt, North Africa, and western Islam from conquest.

The founding of the mendicant orders — Franciscans (1209 CE) and Dominicans (1216 CE) — transformed medieval Catholicism, creating mobile orders of friars who preached in cities, staffed the inquisition, and dominated Europe's universities.

Dante Alighieri was born in 1265 CE — his 'Divine Comedy,' written in the early 14th century, is the supreme literary achievement of medieval Europe, a comprehensive vision of Christian cosmology that defined Italian as a literary language.

The Mongol Pax (Pax Mongolica) that followed the conquests created the conditions for unprecedented Eurasian trade — the Silk Road functioned more safely than ever before, enabling the exchange of goods, peoples, diseases, and ideas across the entire continent.

Kublai Khan (1215–1294 CE), grandson of Genghis, conquered China and founded the Yuan Dynasty — he employed foreign administrators (including Marco Polo), patronized the arts, and attempted failed invasions of Japan and Java.

The Black Death's preconditions developed in the 13th century — the Mongol trade routes that facilitated commerce also created the pathways along which Yersinia pestis, the plague bacterium, would travel westward to devastate Europe in the 1340s.

The Third Council of the Lateran (1215 CE) under Pope Innocent III required Jews to wear distinctive clothing, prohibited them from public office, and mandated separate neighborhoods — institutionalizing the medieval persecution that would mark European Jews for centuries.

The Reconquista advanced dramatically in the 13th century — the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa (1212 CE) broke Almohad power, and by 1250 CE only the emirate of Granada remained under Muslim rule in Iberia.

By 1300 CE, Europe's population had reached perhaps 75 million — the highest it would reach before the Black Death. Cities were growing, trade was expanding, universities were multiplying, and Gothic cathedrals were rising. The great medieval experiment in civilization was at its peak — and on the verge of catastrophe.

centuries.label: 14th Century CE (1300–1399 CE)
centuries.summary: The 14th century is one of catastrophe: the Black Death kills a third of Europe, the Hundred Years' War begins, the papacy moves to Avignon, and the medieval synthesis begins to crack. Yet it also sees Chaucer, Petrarch, and the early stirrings of the Renaissance.

facts.fact: The Black Death (1347–1351 CE) killed between a third and half of Europe's population — approximately 25 million people — in four years. It was the greatest demographic catastrophe in European history, transforming society, economics, and religion permanently.

The Black Death arrived in Europe via Crimean trading posts in 1346 CE — Genoese merchants fleeing the Mongol siege of Caffa may have brought it to Sicily, and it spread with devastating speed along trade routes across the continent.

The psychological impact of the Black Death was immense — the 'Dance of Death' (Danse Macabre) imagery, the flagellant movement, pogroms against Jews blamed for poisoning wells, and a profound questioning of the church's authority all followed in its wake.

The Hundred Years' War (1337–1453 CE) between England and France began over succession disputes — a century of intermittent warfare that defined both nations' identities, produced Joan of Arc, and ended English territorial claims in France.

The Battle of Crécy (1346 CE) demonstrated the devastating effectiveness of the English longbow against French heavy cavalry — the first of several battles that showed medieval knights were no longer the dominant force on the battlefield.

The Avignon Papacy (1309–1377 CE) saw seven popes reside in Avignon rather than Rome under French influence — undermining papal credibility and contributing to the Great Schism (1378–1417 CE) when rival popes simultaneously claimed the throne of St. Peter.

Petrarch (1304–1374 CE), often called the 'first humanist,' revived the study of classical Latin literature and wrote poetry in Italian — his love poems to Laura in the 'Canzoniere' established the sonnet form that would influence Shakespeare.

Giovanni Boccaccio's 'Decameron' (1353 CE), written in the wake of the Black Death, told 100 stories through characters sheltering from the plague — a landmark of Italian literature and a window into 14th-century attitudes toward mortality, sex, and social class.

Geoffrey Chaucer's 'Canterbury Tales' (~1387–1400 CE) is the first great work of English literature — a sophisticated, funny, and psychologically acute portrait of medieval English society told through pilgrims on their way to Thomas Becket's shrine.

The Peasants' Revolt in England (1381 CE) was a massive popular uprising triggered by a poll tax — the rebels briefly controlled London and killed the Archbishop of Canterbury before being suppressed. It reflected the social upheaval the Black Death had caused.

William of Ockham (~1287–1347 CE) developed 'Ockham's Razor' — the principle that the simplest explanation is preferable — and argued for the separation of faith and reason, undermining the Thomistic synthesis and pointing toward empirical science.

The Ottoman Empire was founded by Osman I (~1299 CE) — beginning as a small Anatolian principality, the Ottomans would eventually conquer Constantinople (1453 CE) and become one of the world's great empires.

The Mali Empire under Mansa Musa (1312–1337 CE) was one of the wealthiest states in the world — his famous pilgrimage to Mecca in 1324 CE, during which he gave away so much gold that he depressed Egyptian gold prices for a decade, demonstrated Sub-Saharan Africa's sophistication.

The Yuan Dynasty's collapse in China (1368 CE) and the founding of the Ming Dynasty represented a Chinese rejection of Mongol rule and foreign influence — the Ming would build the Great Wall to its present extent and dispatch Zheng He's great naval expeditions.

John Wycliffe (~1320–1384 CE) argued that the Bible should be available in English and that the church had no legitimate claim to temporal power — his followers (the Lollards) were precursors of the Protestant Reformation, and his ideas influenced Jan Hus in Bohemia.

The Aztec Empire had its origins in this period — the Mexica people settled at Tenochtitlan (modern Mexico City) in 1325 CE, beginning the city that would become the largest in the Americas before the Spanish conquest.

The Bubonic Plague recurred repeatedly after the initial 1347–1351 outbreak — it would return to Europe in waves for the next three centuries, keeping population levels depressed and labor scarce, inadvertently creating conditions for peasant empowerment and wage increases.

The Mongol Empire's dissolution into competing successor states — the Golden Horde, the Ilkhanate, the Chagatai Khanate, and the Yuan Dynasty — ended the Pax Mongolica and disrupted the Eurasian trade routes that had connected East and West.

Timur (Tamerlane, 1336–1405 CE) rose to power in Central Asia, conquering an empire from Turkey to India in campaigns of extraordinary brutality — his sack of Delhi (1398 CE) left 100,000 civilians dead and set back Indian civilization by decades.

By 1400 CE, Europe was a transformed continent — the Black Death had killed millions but inadvertently empowered survivors with higher wages and social mobility, the church's authority was contested, and the humanist movement was beginning to look back to classical antiquity for inspiration rather than medieval theology.

centuries.label: 15th Century CE (1400–1499 CE)
centuries.summary: The 15th century is one of history's great pivots: the fall of Constantinople ends the Byzantine Empire, Gutenberg's printing press transforms communication, Columbus reaches the Americas, and the Italian Renaissance produces Leonardo, Michelangelo, and Botticelli.

facts.fact: The fall of Constantinople to Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II on May 29, 1453 CE ended the Byzantine Empire after 1,100 years — the last Roman emperor, Constantine XI, died fighting on the walls. Greek scholars fleeing to Italy brought classical manuscripts that fueled the Renaissance.

Gutenberg's printing press (~1440–1450 CE) was the most transformative communication technology between writing and the internet — within 50 years of its invention, over 20 million books had been printed across Europe, democratizing access to knowledge.

Christopher Columbus's first voyage to the Americas (1492 CE) initiated the Columbian Exchange — the transfer of plants, animals, diseases, and peoples between hemispheres that transformed global ecology, demographics, and economics more profoundly than any other event.

The Spanish Inquisition, established in 1478 CE, was more about enforcing religious and social conformity than hunting witches — its targets were primarily conversos (Jewish converts to Christianity) suspected of secretly practicing Judaism.

Joan of Arc (1412–1431 CE), an illiterate French peasant girl who claimed divine visions, led French armies to decisive victories in the Hundred Years' War, was captured, tried for heresy, and burned at the stake at 19 — becoming France's greatest national heroine.

The Italian Renaissance reached its peak in Florence under the Medici family — bankers who became the greatest art patrons in history, employing Botticelli, Leonardo, Michelangelo, and Raphael in the most extraordinary concentration of artistic genius in Western history.

Leonardo da Vinci (1452–1519 CE) was the supreme Renaissance figure — painter of the Last Supper and Mona Lisa, designer of flying machines, tanks, and solar power, anatomist, geologist, and botanist. His notebooks contain ideas 300 years ahead of their time.

The Ottoman Empire under Mehmed II (1444–1481 CE) consolidated control over Anatolia and the Balkans, conquered Constantinople, and transformed from a frontier emirate into a sophisticated multi-ethnic empire with a legal system, professional bureaucracy, and patronage of the arts.

The Portuguese Age of Exploration, driven by Prince Henry the Navigator, systematically mapped the African coast — reaching the Cape of Good Hope (1488 CE) and, under Vasco da Gama, India (1498 CE), ending the Islamic monopoly on Eastern trade.

The Aztec Empire under Moctezuma I (1440–1469 CE) dominated central Mexico — Tenochtitlan had a population of 200,000, larger than any European city, with sophisticated agriculture, astronomy, and a writing system, when the Spanish arrived in 1519.

Jan Hus (1369–1415 CE), the Bohemian reformer who built on Wycliffe's ideas, was burned at the Council of Constance after being promised safe conduct — his execution triggered the Hussite Wars, a foretaste of the Reformation's violence a century later.
