The Medici Bank, founded by Giovanni di Bicci de' Medici in 1397 CE, developed double-entry bookkeeping, letters of credit, and branch banking — financial innovations that laid the groundwork for modern capitalism.

Nicolaus Copernicus (1473–1543 CE) was born in this century and was developing his heliocentric theory by its end — his 'De Revolutionibus' (1543 CE) would trigger the Scientific Revolution, but he waited until his deathbed to publish, fearing the reaction.

The Wars of the Roses (1455–1487 CE) in England — a dynastic civil war between the houses of Lancaster and York — ended with the Tudor dynasty's establishment under Henry VII, creating the stable monarchy that would produce Henry VIII and Elizabeth I.

Sandro Botticelli's 'Birth of Venus' (~1484 CE) is emblematic of the Renaissance's rediscovery of classical mythology and the nude human form — commissioned by the Medici, it represents a confident, secular celebration of beauty shocking by medieval standards.

The Songhai Empire dominated West Africa in the 15th century — under Sunni Ali (1464–1492 CE) and Askia the Great, Timbuktu became a major center of Islamic scholarship with a university that attracted scholars from across the Muslim world.

The invention of the printing press had immediate religious consequences — the Bible could now be mass-produced, read by individuals in their vernacular languages, and interpreted without priestly mediation, making the Reformation both possible and inevitable.

Vlad III 'the Impaler' of Wallachia (~1428–1477 CE) — the inspiration for Bram Stoker's Dracula — was a real ruler who resisted Ottoman expansion with extraordinary cruelty, impaling thousands of prisoners as a psychological deterrent.

The expulsion of Jews and Muslims from Spain (1492 CE) — the same year as Columbus — was one of medieval Europe's great tragedies. Spanish Jews (Sephardim) dispersed across the Ottoman Empire, North Africa, and the Netherlands, carrying Iberian culture with them.

By 1500 CE, the world was on the verge of its greatest transformation — the Americas were about to be 'discovered' by Europeans who would conquer, colonize, and devastate indigenous civilizations; the printing press was about to rupture religious unity; and science was about to challenge faith.

centuries.label: 16th Century CE (1500–1599 CE)
centuries.summary: The 16th century reshapes the world: the Protestant Reformation splits Christianity, the Spanish conquer the Americas, Copernicus and Galileo begin the Scientific Revolution, and the Ottoman Empire reaches its peak under Suleiman the Magnificent.

facts.fact: Martin Luther nailed his 95 Theses to the Wittenberg church door on October 31, 1517 CE — a protest against the sale of indulgences that, amplified by the printing press, ignited the Protestant Reformation and permanently fractured Western Christianity.

The Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire under Hernán Cortés (1519–1521 CE) and of the Inca Empire under Francisco Pizarro (1532–1533 CE) were among history's most consequential events — sophisticated civilizations of millions were destroyed within decades.

The Columbian Exchange introduced European diseases — smallpox, measles, influenza — to American populations with no immunity. Estimates suggest 50–90% of indigenous Americans died within a century of contact, the greatest demographic catastrophe in human history.

Copernicus's 'De Revolutionibus' (1543 CE) proposed that the Earth orbits the Sun — the heliocentric model that displaced humanity from the center of the universe and began the Scientific Revolution that would transform humanity's self-understanding.

Henry VIII's break with Rome (1534 CE), motivated primarily by his desire to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon, created the Church of England — a schism driven by dynastic politics that nevertheless triggered genuine religious debate and eventually produced English Protestantism.

The Council of Trent (1545–1563 CE) was the Catholic Church's systematic response to the Protestant Reformation — reaffirming core Catholic doctrines, reforming clerical abuses, and launching the Counter-Reformation that won back much of Central Europe.

Suleiman the Magnificent (1520–1566 CE) ruled the Ottoman Empire at its peak — his armies besieged Vienna twice, controlled the Mediterranean, and his court in Constantinople was a center of law, poetry, and architecture that rivaled anything in Europe.

Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling (1508–1512 CE) and carved the David — the supreme achievements of Renaissance art that represent the ultimate expression of human dignity and beauty in the Western tradition.

The Spanish silver mines of Potosí (Bolivia, discovered 1545 CE) produced such quantities of silver that they caused inflation across Europe — the 'Price Revolution' of the 16th century disrupted economies from England to China, demonstrating the Americas' global economic impact.

John Calvin (1509–1564 CE) established a theocratic republic in Geneva — his theology of predestination and emphasis on discipline, hard work, and individual Bible reading shaped Puritanism, Presbyterianism, and (via Max Weber's argument) the spirit of capitalism.

Ferdinand Magellan's expedition completed the first circumnavigation of the globe (1519–1522 CE), proving Earth's spherical nature and vastness — Magellan himself was killed in the Philippines, but 18 of his 270 men completed the journey.

The Battle of Lepanto (1571 CE) was a decisive naval victory by the Holy League (Spain, Venice, the papacy) over the Ottoman fleet — halting Ottoman expansion in the western Mediterranean and demonstrating that the 'invincible' Ottomans could be defeated.

William Shakespeare (1564–1616 CE) wrote his plays during this century's final decades — his works represent the pinnacle of English literature and a comprehensive exploration of human psychology that remains unsurpassed.

The Elizabethan Age (1558–1603 CE) under Queen Elizabeth I was England's golden age — the defeat of the Spanish Armada (1588 CE), the voyages of Francis Drake, the poetry of Edmund Spenser, and the plays of Marlowe and Shakespeare made England a major European power.

The Mughal Empire, founded by Babur in 1526 CE, became one of the world's great empires under Akbar the Great (1556–1605 CE) — a Muslim ruler who practiced religious tolerance, patronized the arts, and presided over a sophisticated multi-ethnic civilization in India.

Galileo Galilei (1564–1642 CE) was born in this century — his later astronomical observations with the telescope would confirm Copernicus, challenge Aristotle, and bring him into conflict with the Inquisition in the most famous science-versus-religion confrontation in history.

The St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre (1572 CE) — the murder of thousands of French Protestants (Huguenots) in Paris — was the most violent episode of the French Wars of Religion, demonstrating how the Reformation had made sectarian violence a permanent feature of European life.

Francis Bacon (1561–1626 CE) formulated the scientific method — the idea that knowledge should be based on observation, experiment, and induction rather than classical authority — a philosophical revolution that laid the groundwork for modern science.

The Atlantic slave trade expanded dramatically in the 16th century — Portuguese traders initially brought enslaved Africans to sugar plantations in Brazil and the Caribbean, establishing the horrific system that would transport 12 million people across the Atlantic over the next three centuries.

By 1600 CE, the world had been fundamentally transformed — European powers controlled the Americas, the global silver trade connected China to Peru, the Reformation had shattered Christian unity, and the Scientific Revolution was beginning to provide new tools for understanding a universe that was far stranger and larger than anyone had imagined.

centuries.label: 17th Century CE (1600–1699 CE)
centuries.summary: The 17th century is the age of scientific revolution and religious war: Galileo, Newton, Descartes, and Locke transform how humanity understands the world; the Thirty Years' War devastates Central Europe; and England undergoes revolution, regicide, and the birth of constitutional monarchy.

facts.fact: The Thirty Years' War (1618–1648 CE) devastated Central Europe — beginning as a religious conflict between Catholic and Protestant states, it became a struggle for European dominance, killing up to 8 million people and reducing the population of some German regions by a third.

The Peace of Westphalia (1648 CE) ended the Thirty Years' War and established the modern system of sovereign nation-states — the principle that states have supreme authority within their borders, regardless of religious affiliation, is the foundation of modern international relations.

Galileo Galilei's observations with the telescope confirmed Copernicus's heliocentric model, discovered Jupiter's moons, and revealed the Moon's craters — his confrontation with the Inquisition (1633 CE) and forced recantation became the defining symbol of science versus religious authority.

Isaac Newton's 'Principia Mathematica' (1687 CE) unified terrestrial and celestial mechanics — his laws of motion and universal gravitation explained everything from falling apples to planetary orbits, representing perhaps the greatest intellectual achievement in human history.

René Descartes (1596–1650 CE) declared 'I think, therefore I am' — his radical skepticism and dualism of mind and body defined modern philosophy and established the framework of individual rational inquiry that underpins Enlightenment thought.

The English Civil War (1642–1651 CE) pitted Parliament against King Charles I — the king was defeated, tried, and executed in 1649 CE, the first time a European monarch was publicly tried and executed by his own subjects.

The Glorious Revolution (1688 CE) replaced the Catholic James II with the Protestant William of Orange — establishing constitutional monarchy in England, the Bill of Rights (1689 CE), and parliamentary sovereignty that directly influenced the American Constitution.

John Locke (1632–1704 CE) argued that government derives legitimacy from the consent of the governed and must protect natural rights of life, liberty, and property — his political philosophy directly inspired the American Declaration of Independence and French Revolution.

The founding of Jamestown (1607 CE) and Plymouth Colony (1620 CE) established permanent English settlements in North America — the Pilgrim Fathers' voyage on the Mayflower and the Mayflower Compact became founding myths of American identity.

The Tokugawa shogunate (established 1603 CE) unified Japan under a military government that expelled Christian missionaries, closed Japan to foreign trade except through Nagasaki, and enforced a rigid social hierarchy — creating the stable, isolated Japan that persisted until 1868.

The Ottoman siege of Vienna (1683 CE) was the high-water mark of Ottoman expansion into Europe — the failure of the siege and the subsequent Holy League's counteroffensive began the Ottoman Empire's long retreat from Central Europe.

The slave trade reached its catastrophic peak in the 17th century — the Royal African Company (founded 1660 CE) transported hundreds of thousands of enslaved Africans to the Caribbean and Americas, while European powers competed for control of the Atlantic trade.

Baruch Spinoza (1632–1677 CE) argued that God and Nature are one (pantheism), that the Bible was a human document, and that free thought is essential to good government — he was excommunicated by his Jewish community and his works condemned by Catholics, but his ideas deeply influenced the Enlightenment.

The Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb (1658–1707 CE) reversed Akbar's religious tolerance, imposing Islamic law on India's Hindu majority and destroying temples — triggering rebellions that weakened the Mughal Empire and eventually enabled British conquest.

The Scientific Revolution produced not just new knowledge but a new method — the Royal Society (founded 1660 CE) institutionalized peer review, experimental verification, and the publication of results, creating the social structure of modern science.
The Great Fire of London (1666 CE) destroyed 13,000 houses and 87 churches — its rebuilding by Christopher Wren, including St. Paul's Cathedral, created the architectural framework of modern London.

Blaise Pascal (1623–1662 CE) pioneered probability theory, invented the mechanical calculator, and wrote the 'Pensées' — his famous 'wager' argued it is rational to believe in God since the potential gain (eternal life) outweighs the cost of belief.

The Ming Dynasty collapsed in 1644 CE and was replaced by the Qing (Manchu) Dynasty — foreign conquerors who required Chinese men to wear the Manchu queue (braid) as a sign of submission but who largely adopted Chinese culture and governance.

The Dutch Golden Age (1580–1672 CE) saw the Netherlands become the world's leading trading and financial power — the Dutch East India Company (VOC) was the world's first multinational corporation, and Rembrandt, Vermeer, and Spinoza all flourished in Amsterdam.

By 1700 CE, Europe had achieved decisive military and economic advantages over other world civilizations — gunpowder weapons, oceanic navigation, and capitalist finance were giving European powers the tools to dominate the globe over the next two centuries.

centuries.label: 18th Century CE (1700–1799 CE)
centuries.summary: The 18th century is the Age of Enlightenment and Revolution: Voltaire, Rousseau, and Hume challenge authority; the American and French Revolutions remake politics; the Industrial Revolution begins; and James Cook maps the Pacific.

facts.fact: The Enlightenment was an 18th-century intellectual movement that subjected tradition, religion, and authority to rational scrutiny — Voltaire, Hume, Rousseau, Kant, and Diderot argued that reason, not revelation, should guide human affairs.

The American Declaration of Independence (1776 CE), largely written by Thomas Jefferson, declared that 'all men are created equal' and possess inalienable rights of life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness — applying Locke's philosophy to create a new political order.

The French Revolution (1789 CE) overthrew the monarchy, declared the Rights of Man, and executed King Louis XVI — initiating a decade of radical politics, the Terror, and eventually Napoleon, that permanently transformed European politics.

The Reign of Terror (1793–1794 CE) killed approximately 40,000 people under Robespierre's Committee of Public Safety — demonstrating how revolutionary idealism could produce political violence, a paradox that haunted 19th and 20th century radicalism.

James Watt's improved steam engine (1769 CE) began the Industrial Revolution — the shift from human and animal power to fossil fuel energy that would transform human civilization more profoundly than any event since the agricultural revolution.

Adam Smith's 'Wealth of Nations' (1776 CE) — published the same year as the American Declaration — argued that free markets, specialization, and the 'invisible hand' of self-interest produce prosperity more efficiently than government control. It became the bible of capitalism.

Immanuel Kant (1724–1804 CE) argued that the mind actively structures experience rather than passively receiving it — his 'Critique of Pure Reason' (1781 CE) is perhaps the most important work of modern philosophy, synthesizing rationalism and empiricism.

Voltaire (1694–1778 CE) was the Enlightenment's most famous polemicist — his satirical novel 'Candide' (1759 CE) mocked religious optimism, his attacks on the Catholic Church's power and intolerance made him the symbol of freethought across Europe.

Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712–1778 CE) argued in the 'Social Contract' that legitimate government requires the consent of the governed and reflects the 'general will' — his ideas inspired both democratic republicanism and, in their collectivist form, later totalitarianism.

The Seven Years' War (1756–1763 CE) was the first truly global conflict — fought across Europe, North America, the Caribbean, West Africa, India, and the Philippines, it established British dominance and set the stage for American independence.

Captain James Cook's three voyages (1768–1779 CE) completed the mapping of the Pacific Ocean — encountering Australia, New Zealand, Hawaii, and dozens of Pacific islands, making the first sustained European contact with Polynesian civilizations.

The Haitian Revolution (1791–1804 CE) was the only successful slave revolt in history — enslaved Africans overthrew French colonial rule in Saint-Domingue to create Haiti, the world's first Black republic, sending shockwaves through slaveholding societies worldwide.

David Hume (1711–1776 CE) was the most radical of Enlightenment skeptics — arguing against miracles, the immortality of the soul, and a providential God, he established empiricism as the foundation of modern philosophy of science.

Napoleon Bonaparte rose from Corsican obscurity to become Emperor of France (1804 CE) — his legal code (the Code Napoleon), continental conquests, and eventual defeat reshaped Europe's boundaries, institutions, and nationalist movements.

The abolition movement began in earnest in the 18th century — Quakers banned slaveholding among members (1776 CE), and the Society for Effecting the Abolition of the Slave Trade was founded in London (1787 CE), beginning the campaign that ended British slavery in 1833.

Textiles industrialized first in England — Hargreaves's spinning jenny (1764), Arkwright's water frame (1769), and Cartwright's power loom (1784) transformed textile production from cottage craft to factory system, beginning urbanization and the proletariat.

The United States Constitution (1787 CE) created the first modern democratic republic — its separation of powers, federalism, and Bill of Rights (1791 CE) established a template for constitutional government that influenced democracies worldwide.

Edward Jenner developed the smallpox vaccine in 1796 CE — the first vaccine in history, beginning the era of immunization that would eventually eliminate diseases that had killed tens of millions throughout history.

The Encyclopédie, edited by Diderot and d'Alembert (1751–1772 CE), compiled the knowledge of the Enlightenment into 35 volumes — a deliberate project to democratize knowledge and undermine the authority of church and aristocracy.

By 1800 CE, the world was transforming at unprecedented speed — the Industrial Revolution was beginning to generate wealth on a scale never before seen, democratic ideas were threatening traditional hierarchies, and European empires were extending their reach across the globe in ways that would define the next century.

centuries.label: 19th Century CE (1800–1899 CE)
centuries.summary: The 19th century is the age of nationalism, industrialization, imperialism, and Darwin: Europe colonizes the world, the United States tears itself apart over slavery, Marx predicts revolution, and Victorian science transforms humanity's understanding of its origins.

facts.fact: Napoleon's defeat at Waterloo (1815 CE) and the Congress of Vienna restored European monarchies but could not undo the French Revolution's ideas — nationalism, liberalism, and the concept of popular sovereignty would drive European revolutions in 1830 and 1848.

The Industrial Revolution transformed Britain first, then Europe and America — by 1850 CE, Britain produced half the world's iron and cotton, its railways connected its cities, and the factory system had created both unprecedented wealth and a new urban working class living in squalor.

Charles Darwin's 'On the Origin of Species' (1859 CE) established evolution by natural selection — one of the most consequential scientific ideas in history, it transformed biology, challenged religious accounts of creation, and (when misapplied) provided pseudo-scientific justification for racism and imperialism.

The American Civil War (1861–1865 CE) killed 620,000 soldiers — more Americans than died in all other American wars combined — and ended slavery in the United States. Lincoln's Emancipation Proclamation (1863 CE) made abolition the war's explicit goal.

Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels published the 'Communist Manifesto' (1848 CE) — arguing that history is driven by class struggle and that capitalism would inevitably be overthrown by the proletariat, creating a classless society. It became the most influential political document of the 20th century.

The abolition of slavery in the British Empire (1833 CE), then the US (1865 CE), then Brazil (1888 CE) ended the transatlantic slave trade after 400 years — but the racism that had justified slavery persisted and took new forms in Jim Crow laws and colonial ideology.

The Scramble for Africa (1881–1914 CE) saw European powers partition the entire African continent among themselves — by 1914 CE, only Ethiopia and Liberia remained independent, and Africa's colonial borders (drawn without regard to ethnic or political realities) created problems that persist today.

The Opium Wars (1839–1842 CE, 1856–1860 CE) forced China to open its ports to British trade — the Treaty of Nanking ceded Hong Kong to Britain and forced 'unequal treaties' on China that humiliated the Qing Dynasty and fueled Chinese nationalism.

The Indian Rebellion of 1857 CE ('Indian Mutiny' or 'First War of Independence') ended the East India Company's rule — the British Crown took direct control of India, beginning the era of the British Raj that lasted until 1947.

The unification of Germany (1871 CE) under Bismarck's Prussia created Europe's most powerful new state — the German Empire's industrial and military strength would destabilize European balance of power and contribute to both World Wars.

The germ theory of disease, developed by Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch in the 1860s–1880s, explained that infectious diseases are caused by microorganisms — a discovery that transformed medicine, enabled vaccination, and dramatically increased human life expectancy.

The Italian Risorgimento unified Italy under the House of Savoy (1861 CE) — Giuseppe Garibaldi's 'Thousand Redshirts' captured Sicily and Naples, while Cavour's diplomacy secured French support, creating modern Italy from a patchwork of kingdoms and papal territories.

The construction of the transcontinental railroad in America (completed 1869 CE), primarily by Chinese and Irish immigrant laborers, connected the continent and enabled westward expansion — at the cost of displacing and destroying Native American peoples and cultures.

Friedrich Nietzsche (1844–1900 CE) declared 'God is dead' — not as celebration but as diagnosis, arguing that the collapse of Christian metaphysics left Western civilization without a foundation for values, necessitating the creation of new ones.

Sigmund Freud developed psychoanalysis in the 1890s CE — his theories of the unconscious, repression, and the Oedipus complex transformed how Western culture understood the human mind, sexuality, and the irrational forces driving behavior.

The Russian serfs were emancipated in 1861 CE by Tsar Alexander II — but the liberation was incomplete, burdening freed serfs with redemption payments for land they'd worked for generations, fueling the resentment that would produce the 1917 Revolution.

The Meiji Restoration (1868 CE) transformed Japan from a feudal society to a modern industrial state in a single generation — Japan adopted Western technology, law, and military organization with astonishing speed, becoming the first non-Western great power.

James Clerk Maxwell's equations (1865 CE) unified electricity, magnetism, and light as aspects of a single electromagnetic phenomenon — among the most beautiful achievements in physics, they enabled radio, television, and modern telecommunications.

The Dreyfus Affair (1894–1906 CE) — the wrongful conviction of a Jewish French officer on treason charges — exposed virulent French antisemitism and convinced Theodor Herzl that Jews needed their own state, leading to the founding of modern Zionism.

By 1900 CE, European empires controlled 84% of the world's land surface — the technological, military, and economic advantages of industrialization had given a handful of Western nations dominance over the rest of humanity, creating a global order of colonizer and colonized whose consequences still shape our world.

The Opium Wars (1839-1842 and 1856-1860) forced China to open its markets to British trade and cede Hong Kong — representing the height of European imperial arrogance, where Britain literally went to war to force China to accept British drug dealers.

The abolition of slavery across the British Empire (1833) and the end of the Atlantic slave trade marked Christianity's moral triumph over economic interests — evangelical Christians like William Wilberforce had successfully argued that slavery contradicted Christian teachings about human dignity.

The Crimean War (1853-1856) was the first war extensively photographed and reported by telegraph — Florence Nightingale's nursing reforms and the charge of the Light Brigade became legendary, while the conflict exposed the incompetence of European military leadership in the industrial age.

The opening of the Suez Canal (1869) cut 4,000 miles off the sea route between Europe and Asia — this French engineering triumph transformed global trade but also intensified European competition for control of trade routes, accelerating the 'Scramble for Africa.'

The Indian Rebellion of 1857 (the 'Sepoy Mutiny') nearly ended British rule in India when native soldiers revolted against cartridges greased with cow and pig fat — offensive to both Hindu and Muslim religious beliefs — forcing Britain to abolish the East India Company and impose direct imperial rule.

centuries.label: Early 20th Century (1900–1945 CE)
centuries.summary: The first half of the 20th century is defined by catastrophe on an unprecedented scale: two World Wars, the Russian Revolution, the Holocaust, and the atomic bomb — events that killed over 100 million people and remade the world order entirely.

facts.fact: World War I (1914–1918 CE) killed 17 million people and destroyed four empires — the German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian — in four years of industrial slaughter. The war's causes, course, and consequences shape the modern world more than any other event.

The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo (June 28, 1914 CE) triggered a chain of alliance obligations that plunged Europe into war within six weeks — demonstrating how a system of interlocking alliances can make local conflicts global catastrophes.

The Russian Revolution (1917 CE) overthrew the tsar in February, then brought the Bolsheviks to power in October — Lenin's coup created the world's first communist state, triggering a civil war, a Red Terror, and eventually the Soviet Union.

The Treaty of Versailles (1919 CE) imposed harsh reparations and territorial losses on Germany — the 'war guilt' clause and economic devastation created the resentment that Hitler exploited, making Versailles a direct cause of World War II.

The influenza pandemic of 1918–1919 CE killed 50–100 million people worldwide — more than World War I itself, and possibly the deadliest single event in human history. It infected a third of the world's population and disproportionately killed healthy young adults.

The rise of fascism in Italy (Mussolini, 1922 CE) and Nazi Germany (Hitler, 1933 CE) represented a new form of totalitarianism — ultranationalist, violently antisemitic, and contemptuous of democracy, promising national rebirth through violence and racial purity.

The Holocaust (1941–1945 CE) was the systematic murder of six million Jews and millions of others (Roma, disabled people, LGBTQ+ people, Slavs, political opponents) by Nazi Germany — industrialized genocide using gas chambers, a crime against humanity with no precedent.

World War II (1939–1945 CE) killed approximately 70–85 million people — the deadliest conflict in human history, fought across Europe, Asia, Africa, and the Pacific, involving nearly every nation on Earth.

The Battle of Britain (1940 CE) was the first military campaign fought entirely in the air — the RAF's successful defense against the Luftwaffe preserved British independence and Churchill's determination to continue fighting, preventing a Nazi-dominated Europe.

Operation Barbarossa (June 1941 CE), Germany's invasion of the Soviet Union, was the largest military operation in history — eventually costing Germany the war, as the USSR absorbed devastating losses but fought back with a ferocity that ground down the Wehrmacht.

The Manhattan Project produced the atomic bomb — tested at Trinity, New Mexico in July 1945 CE — and the bombs dropped on Hiroshima (August 6) and Nagasaki (August 9) killed 130,000–226,000 people and ended World War II in the Pacific.

The Great Depression (1929–1939 CE) was triggered by the Wall Street Crash and spread globally — unemployment reached 25% in America, destroying the economic and political stability that might have prevented Hitler's rise and the second World War.

Stalin's collectivization of Soviet agriculture (1929–1933 CE) and the Holodomor — a famine that killed 3.5–5 million Ukrainians — was a man-made catastrophe resulting from forced collectivization, proving that communist ideology could produce mass murder on par with fascism.

Mahatma Gandhi's non-violent resistance movement (satyagraha) challenged British rule in India — his Salt March (1930 CE), civil disobedience campaigns, and moral authority made him the most influential figure in the anti-colonial movement.

Albert Einstein's special relativity (1905 CE) and general relativity (1915 CE) overthrew Newtonian physics — redefining space, time, and gravity in ways that GPS technology, nuclear energy, and cosmology all depend on today.

The Chinese Communist Revolution, following decades of warlordism, Japanese invasion, and civil war, brought Mao Zedong to power in 1949 CE — the People's Republic of China was proclaimed on October 1, establishing a communist state of 550 million people.

The Nuremberg Trials (1945–1946 CE) prosecuted Nazi leaders for war crimes and crimes against humanity — establishing the principle that individuals can be held accountable under international law, a foundation of the modern human rights framework.

Penicillin, discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928 CE and developed by Florey and Chain in the 1940s, was the first antibiotic — transforming medicine and saving hundreds of millions of lives from bacterial infections that had previously been death sentences.

The United Nations was founded in 1945 CE by 51 nations in the aftermath of World War II — embodying the hope that international institutions could prevent future wars, a hope partly realized and partly not, as the Cold War began almost immediately.

By 1945 CE, the old European empires were fatally weakened, America and the Soviet Union had emerged as superpowers, the atomic bomb had made total war between great powers suicidal, and the world was about to embark on the Cold War — the defining geopolitical contest of the century's second half.

centuries.label: Late 20th Century (1945–1999 CE)
centuries.summary: The second half of the 20th century is shaped by the Cold War, decolonization, the civil rights movement, the digital revolution, and the collapse of the Soviet Union — an era of both nuclear terror and extraordinary human progress.

facts.fact: The Cold War (1947–1991 CE) divided the world between American-led capitalism and Soviet-led communism — a geopolitical contest that never became direct military conflict between the superpowers but produced proxy wars, nuclear terror, and an arms race that consumed vast resources.

The Marshall Plan (1948 CE) provided $13 billion in American aid to rebuild Western Europe after World War II — one of the most successful foreign policy initiatives in history, creating the prosperous, stable democracies that became America's Cold War allies.

The founding of Israel (1948 CE) fulfilled the Zionist dream but immediately triggered the Arab-Israeli War — Palestinian Arabs call 1948 the Nakba ('catastrophe'), as 700,000 Palestinians were displaced, a conflict whose wounds remain open today.

India's independence and partition (1947 CE) created two nations — India and Pakistan — along religious lines, triggering one of history's largest mass migrations (15 million people) and communal violence that killed up to 2 million people.

The Korean War (1950–1953 CE) was the first 'hot' conflict of the Cold War — American-led UN forces and Chinese troops fought to a stalemate, dividing the peninsula at the 38th parallel. The armistice that ended fighting left Korea divided, as it remains today.

The Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962 CE) brought the world closest to nuclear war — Soviet missiles in Cuba, discovered by American reconnaissance, triggered 13 days of superpower confrontation that ended with Soviet withdrawal in exchange for US pledges not to invade Cuba.

The Civil Rights Movement in America, led by Martin Luther King Jr., used non-violent resistance to challenge racial segregation — the March on Washington (1963 CE), the Civil Rights Act (1964 CE), and the Voting Rights Act (1965 CE) dismantled the legal architecture of American apartheid.

The Vietnam War (1955–1975 CE) was America's defining Cold War trauma — 58,000 Americans and 2 million Vietnamese died in a conflict that ended in American defeat, shattered domestic consensus, and produced the 'Vietnam syndrome' of reluctance to commit military force.

The decolonization of Africa and Asia (1945–1975 CE) ended European empires — dozens of new nations gained independence, but colonial borders drawn without regard to ethnic realities, resource extraction, and Cold War interference left many in poverty and conflict.

The moon landing (July 20, 1969 CE) — Neil Armstrong's first steps on the Moon during Apollo 11 — was the greatest human technological achievement and the culmination of the Space Race between America and the Soviet Union.

The Cultural Revolution in China (1966–1976 CE) under Mao Zedong mobilized youth to destroy 'bourgeois' culture — temples, books, and artifacts were destroyed, intellectuals were persecuted, and an estimated 500,000–2 million people died in political violence.

The collapse of the Soviet Union (1991 CE) — the most consequential geopolitical event since World War II — ended the Cold War, liberated Eastern Europe, and left America as the world's sole superpower, raising hopes for a 'unipolar moment' of democratic peace.

The invention of the internet — ARPANET (1969 CE) evolving into the World Wide Web (Tim Berners-Lee, 1989 CE) — began the Information Revolution that would transform communication, commerce, politics, and human social life as profoundly as the printing press.

The HIV/AIDS pandemic, identified in 1981 CE, killed over 35 million people by the early 21st century — disproportionately affecting gay men, Africans, and intravenous drug users, it exposed both medical ignorance and social prejudice before antiretroviral drugs transformed it into a manageable condition.

The fall of the Berlin Wall (November 9, 1989 CE) symbolized the end of communist Eastern Europe — images of jubilant Germans tearing down the wall were among the most powerful of the 20th century, marking the end of Europe's division.

The Rwandan Genocide (1994 CE) killed approximately 800,000 Tutsis and moderate Hutus in 100 days — the international community's failure to intervene despite clear warning became a defining moment of shame and produced the 'Responsibility to Protect' doctrine in international law.

The Human Genome Project, completed in 2003 CE (begun in 1990), sequenced the entire human DNA — opening the era of genomic medicine, gene therapy, and ethical debates about genetic engineering that continue today.

The 1990s saw unprecedented economic globalization — the World Trade Organization (1995 CE), NAFTA, and China's integration into world trade created a global economy that lifted hundreds of millions from poverty while also producing inequality, environmental damage, and political backlash.

Nelson Mandela's release from prison (1990 CE) and election as South Africa's president (1994 CE) ended apartheid through negotiated transition rather than civil war — his model of reconciliation over revenge remains one of the 20th century's most inspiring political achievements.

By 2000 CE, global life expectancy had risen from 47 years in 1950 to 66 years, child mortality had fallen dramatically, and extreme poverty had declined significantly — the second half of the 20th century saw the greatest improvement in human welfare in history, even amid its unprecedented violence.

centuries.label: 21st Century (2000–present)
centuries.summary: The 21st century is defined by the aftermath of 9/11, the rise of China, the digital revolution, climate change, and a global pandemic — an era in which the certainties of the post-Cold War order have dissolved and the shape of the future remains deeply uncertain.

facts.fact: The September 11, 2001 attacks — Al-Qaeda hijackers killed 2,977 people in New York, Washington, and Pennsylvania — triggered the Global War on Terror, the invasions of Afghanistan and Iraq, and a reshaping of American foreign policy and civil liberties that continues today.

The invasion of Iraq (2003 CE) by the US-led coalition, based on false intelligence about weapons of mass destruction, toppled Saddam Hussein but created a power vacuum that produced ISIS and regional chaos — widely regarded as one of the greatest foreign policy blunders of the modern era.

China's economic rise has been the most consequential geopolitical development of the 21st century — growing from the world's 6th largest economy in 2000 to the 2nd largest by 2010, lifting 800 million people from poverty in the most rapid economic transformation in history.

The 2008 global financial crisis — triggered by the collapse of the US subprime mortgage market — was the worst economic disaster since the Great Depression, requiring massive government bailouts of banks and producing a decade of austerity, inequality, and political polarization.

The Arab Spring (2010–2012 CE) brought mass protests that toppled governments in Tunisia, Egypt, Libya, and Yemen — initially celebrated as a democratic awakening, it mostly produced civil wars, authoritarian retrenchment, and the rise of ISIS in Syria and Iraq.

The Syrian Civil War (began 2011 CE) produced the worst refugee crisis since World War II — over 13 million Syrians were displaced, 6.6 million as refugees, contributing to the migration crisis that fueled populist nationalism across Europe.

The smartphone revolution — Apple's iPhone launched in 2007 CE — put the internet in everyone's pocket, transforming social interaction, commerce, politics, and attention spans. By 2020, over 3.5 billion people owned smartphones.

The rise of social media — Facebook (2004 CE), Twitter (2006 CE), YouTube (2005 CE), Instagram (2010 CE) — transformed political communication, enabled Arab Spring organizing, spread misinformation, amplified polarization, and created new forms of surveillance capitalism.

CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technology, developed by Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier (Nobel Prize 2020 CE), allows precise editing of DNA — opening possibilities for curing genetic diseases and raising profound ethical questions about human genetic engineering.

Climate change emerged as the defining long-term challenge of the 21st century — the Paris Agreement (2015 CE) committed nearly every nation to reducing carbon emissions, but implementation has fallen far short of what scientists say is necessary to avoid catastrophic warming.

The COVID-19 pandemic (2020–2023 CE) killed over 7 million people officially (likely 15–20 million excess deaths) and disrupted global society more profoundly than any event since World War II — accelerating remote work, e-commerce, and vaccine development while exposing deep inequalities.

mRNA vaccines, developed with unprecedented speed during COVID-19 (Pfizer/BioNTech and Moderna, 2020 CE), represented a revolution in vaccine technology — the same platform is now being developed for cancer, HIV, and other diseases.

The rise of artificial intelligence — particularly large language models like GPT-4 and Claude, and image generators like Midjourney — beginning around 2022 CE represents a potential transformation of intellectual labor as significant as the Industrial Revolution was to physical labor.

Russia's invasion of Ukraine (February 24, 2022 CE) was the largest European land war since World War II — challenging the post-Cold War order, producing a severe humanitarian crisis, and revitalizing NATO while raising fears of nuclear escalation.

The #MeToo movement (2017 CE) produced a global reckoning with sexual harassment and assault — high-profile prosecutions of Harvey Weinstein and others, and the cultural shift it represented, were part of a broader 21st-century reassessment of gender, power, and accountability.

India surpassed China as the world's most populous nation in 2023 CE — with 1.4 billion people, a growing middle class, and the world's largest democracy, India's 21st-century trajectory will significantly shape global politics and economics.

The Human Genome Project's completion enabled 23andMe, Ancestry DNA, and similar services to make genetic ancestry testing widely available — reconnecting millions of people with their origins, including descendants of enslaved Africans who had lost knowledge of their heritage.

Renewable energy — solar and wind power — has fallen in cost by over 90% since 2010 CE, making clean energy economically competitive with fossil fuels for the first time — a technological transition that may be humanity's best hope for addressing climate change.

The decoding of ancient DNA — from Neanderthals (2010 CE), Denisovans, and ancient human populations — has revolutionized our understanding of human prehistory, revealing interbreeding between Homo sapiens and other human species that Bart Ehrman's generation of scholars never imagined possible.

As of 2024 CE, humanity faces an unprecedented convergence of challenges — climate change, artificial intelligence, nuclear proliferation, democratic backsliding, and pandemic risk — alongside extraordinary opportunities in medicine, clean energy, and human connection. The outcome of these tensions will determine the shape of human civilization for centuries.
